5. From the throne came out flashes of lightning and roaring and crashes of thunder. Seven flaming torches, which are the seven spirits of God, were burning in front of the throne,
6. and in front of the throne was something like a sea of glass, like crystal. In the middle of the throne and around the throne were four living creatures full of eyes in front and in back.
7. The first living creature was like a lion, the second creature like an ox, the third creature had a face like a man’s, and the fourth creature looked like an eagle flying.
8. Each one of the four living creatures had six wings and was full of eyes all around and inside. They never rest day or night, saying: “Holy, Holy, Holy is the Lord God, the All-Powerful, Who was, and who is, and who is still to come!”
9. And whenever the living creatures give glory, honor, and thanks to the one who sits on the throne, who lives forever and ever,
10. the twenty-four elders throw themselves to the ground before the one who sits on the throne and worship the one who lives forever and ever, and they offer their crowns before his throne, saying:
11. “You are worthy, our Lord and God, to receive glory and honor and power, since you created all things, and because of your will they existed and were created!”
4.WEB:
1. After these things I looked and saw a door opened in heaven, and the first voice that I heard, like a trumpet speaking with me, was one saying, “Come up here, and I will show you the things which must happen after this.”
2. Immediately I was in the Spirit. Behold, there was a throne set in heaven, and one sitting on the throne
3. that looked like a jasper stone and a sardius. There was a rainbow around the throne, like an emerald to look at.
4. Around the throne were twenty-four thrones. On the thrones were twenty-four elders sitting, dressed in white garments, with crowns of gold on their heads.
5. Out of the throne proceed lightnings, sounds, and thunders. There were seven lamps of fire burning before his throne, which are the seven Spirits of God.
6. Before the throne was something like a sea of glass, similar to crystal. In the middle of the throne, and around the throne were four living creatures full of eyes before and behind.
7. The first creature was like a lion, and the second creature like a calf, and the third creature had a face like a man, and the fourth was like a flying eagle.
8. The four living creatures, each one of them having six wings, are full of eyes around and within. They have no rest day and night, saying, “Holy, holy, holy is the Lord God, the Almighty, who was and who is and who is to come!”
9. When the living creatures give glory, honor, and thanks to him who sits on the throne, to him who lives forever and ever,
10. the twenty-four elders fall down before him who sits on the throne, and worship him who lives forever and ever, and throw their crowns before the throne, saying,
11. “Worthy are you, our Lord and God, the Holy One, to receive the glory, the honor, and the power, for you created all things, and because of your desire they existed, and were created!”
4.notes:
Revelation - Chapter 4
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
John is caught up to heaven's throne room — God sits on a throne surrounded by twenty-four elders, four living creatures, lightning and thunder, and ceaseless worship: "Holy, holy, holy, Lord God Almighty, which was, and is, and is to come."


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: The Apostle John (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 4 begins the third section of Revelation — "the
things which shall be hereafter" (1:19). John is caught up to heaven through
an open door (v.1) and sees the throne room of God. This is the control center
of the universe — where all authority resides and from which all judgments
proceed. Chapters 4-5 form a unit: chapter 4 focuses on God the Creator on
His throne; chapter 5 focuses on Christ the Redeemer and the sealed scroll.

The Throne (vv.1-3): A door opened in heaven (v.1). A throne with One seated
on it — described in gemstone imagery: jasper (clear brilliance), sardine/
carnelian (fiery red), emerald rainbow (green — mercy) (vv.2-3).

The Court (vv.4-6a): Twenty-four elders on thrones, in white, with gold
crowns (v.4). Lightning, thunder, voices from the throne (v.5). Seven lamps
= the seven Spirits of God (v.5). A sea of glass like crystal (v.6a).

The Living Creatures (vv.6b-8): Four living creatures full of eyes — lion,
calf/ox, man, eagle (vv.6b-7). Six wings each (v.8). Ceaseless worship:
"Holy, holy, holy, Lord God Almighty, which was, and is, and is to come" (v.8).

Worship (vv.9-11): When the creatures worship, the elders fall down, cast
their crowns before the throne, and declare God worthy because He created all
things (vv.9-11).


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/revelation-4/
 Guzik covers the heavenly door, the throne, the elders, the living
 creatures, and the worship of God as Creator.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "Holy, Holy, Holy" on v.8 (MTP Vol. 8, No. 446)
 "Holy, holy, holy — three times, never two, never four — three. The
 triune God is thrice holy. And this is the ceaseless song of heaven — not
 'love, love, love' (though He is love) but 'holy, holy, holy.' Holiness
 is the attribute that governs all others. God's love is holy love. His
 justice is holy justice. His mercy is holy mercy. Heaven's first and
 constant declaration: He is holy."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1               | KJV         | "after this" — Greek "meta tauta" — after these things. Marks the transition from "things which are" (churches) to "things hereafter" (future events).
v.1               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "meta tauta" — after these things. Marks the transition from "things which are" (churches) to "things hereafter" (future events).
v.4               | KJV         | "four and twenty elders" — Identity debated: (1) representatives of the redeemed (12 tribes + 12 apostles), (2) an angelic order, (3) OT and NT saints combined. Their white robes and crowns suggest redeemed humans.
v.4               | ESV         | "twenty-four elders" — Identity debated: (1) representatives of the redeemed (12 tribes + 12 apostles), (2) an angelic order, (3) OT and NT saints combined. Their white robes and crowns suggest redeemed humans.
v.6               | KJV         | "beasts" — Greek "zōa" — living creatures (not "thēria" = wild beasts). ESV correctly translates the Greek; KJV's "beasts" is misleading in modern English. These are exalted beings, not monsters. Similar to Ezekiel's cherubim and Isaiah's seraphim.
v.6               | ESV         | "living creatures" — Greek "zōa" — living creatures (not "thēria" = wild beasts). ESV correctly translates the Greek; KJV's "beasts" is misleading in modern English. These are exalted beings, not monsters. Similar to Ezekiel's cherubim and Isaiah's seraphim.
v.8               | KJV         | "Holy, holy, holy" — The trisagion (thrice-holy). The only attribute of God repeated three times in Scripture. Emphasizes His absolute, infinite, transcendent holiness.
v.8               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — The trisagion (thrice-holy). The only attribute of God repeated three times in Scripture. Emphasizes His absolute, infinite, transcendent holiness.
v.11              | KJV         | "for thy pleasure they are and were created" — Greek "dia to thelēma sou" — because of Your will. KJV's "pleasure" emphasizes God's delight; ESV's "will" emphasizes God's sovereign choice. Both are valid renderings of "thelēma." All things exist because God willed them into existence. Creation is an act of divine will and pleasure.
v.11              | ESV         | "by your will they existed and were created" — Greek "dia to thelēma sou" — because of Your will. KJV's "pleasure" emphasizes God's delight; ESV's "will" emphasizes God's sovereign choice. Both are valid renderings of "thelēma." All things exist because God willed them into existence. Creation is an act of divine will and pleasure.
v.1               | NET         | "After these things I looked, and there was a door standing open in heaven!" — "standing open" (aneOgmenE = opened and remaining open — perfect participle). WEB: "a door opened in heaven." The door opened for John gives readers access to heaven's perspective.
v.3               | NET         | "the one seated on it was like jasper and carnelian in appearance" — "jasper" (iaspidi = likely diamond/clear crystal) and "carnelian" (sardiO = blood-red stone). WEB: "like a jasper stone and a sardius." God described in reflected light, not direct form — no anthropomorphism.
v.6               | NET         | "In the middle of the throne and around the throne were four living creatures full of eyes in front and in back" — "living creatures" (zOa = living ones — not thEria/ beasts). WEB: "four living creatures." KJV: "four beasts." NET/WEB correctly avoid "beasts" — these are exalted beings, not animals.
v.8               | NET         | "Holy, Holy, Holy is the Lord God, the All-Powerful, Who was and who is, and who is still to come!" — the trisagion (thrice-holy). WEB: "Holy, holy, holy is the Lord God, the Almighty." KJV: "Holy, holy, holy, Lord God Almighty." Echoing Isaiah 6:3 — ceaseless worship (they never stop, day or night).
v.11              | NET         | "You are worthy, our Lord and God, to receive glory and honor and power, since you created all things" — "you created" (ektisas = you brought into being). WEB: "You created all things." Creation as the ground of worship — God is worshipped because He is Creator.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Throne            | The central symbol of Revelation. God's sovereign authority                                                        | 
Twenty-four elders| Representatives of the redeemed community. Crowned,                                                                | 
Four living creatures| Exalted beings surrounding God's throne. Lion                                                                      | 
Holy, holy, holy  | The trisagion. God's supreme attribute declared                                                                    | 
Sea of glass      | Calm, crystal-clear expanse before the throne. Suggests                                                            | 
Cast their crowns | The elders' response to God's worthiness: returning                                                                | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "A throne was set in heaven" (v.2). God is on His throne — sovereign,
   ruling, in control. Whatever chaos exists on earth, heaven's throne is
   stable. Does this give you peace?

2. "Holy, holy, holy" (v.8). Heaven's primary declaration is God's
   holiness. Do you take His holiness seriously? Does it produce reverence,
   awe, and worship in you?

3. The elders "cast their crowns before the throne" (v.10). Whatever honors
   or achievements you have — they belong to God. Are you willing to lay
   them at His feet? Or do you cling to your crowns?

4. "For thy pleasure they are and were created" (v.11). You exist because
   God willed it — for His pleasure. Does knowing you were created for
   God's pleasure change how you view your life's purpose?

5. This chapter is entirely about worship. How does your worship compare to
   heaven's? Is it ceaseless, passionate, God-centered? Or occasional,
   routine, self-centered?

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Revelation Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/revelation/

David Guzik — Revelation 4 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/revelation-4/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

5.notes:
Revelation - Chapter 5
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A scroll sealed with seven seals and no one worthy to open it — until the Lion of Judah, the Lamb who was slain, takes the scroll, and all heaven erupts: "Worthy is the Lamb that was slain to receive power, and riches, and wisdom."


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: The Apostle John (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 5 continues the throne room scene from chapter 4.
The focus shifts from God the Creator (ch.4) to Christ the Redeemer (ch.5).
A sealed scroll appears — representing God's plan for history/judgment — and
no one is worthy to open it. John weeps until the Lion of Judah (who is also
the slain Lamb) steps forward. This chapter contains some of the most
magnificent worship in all of Scripture.

The Sealed Scroll (vv.1-4): A scroll sealed with seven seals in God's right
hand (v.1). A strong angel asks: "Who is worthy to open the book?" (v.2). No
one in heaven, earth, or under the earth is found worthy (v.3). John weeps
greatly (v.4).

The Lion Who Is a Lamb (vv.5-7): An elder says: "The Lion of the tribe of
Juda, the Root of David, hath prevailed" (v.5). But when John looks, he sees
not a lion but "a Lamb as it had been slain" (v.6) — with seven horns
(complete power) and seven eyes (complete knowledge/Spirit). The Lamb takes
the scroll (v.7).

The New Song (vv.8-10): The creatures and elders fall before the Lamb (v.8).
Golden vials of incense = the prayers of saints (v.8). They sing: "Thou art
worthy... for thou wast slain, and hast redeemed us to God by thy blood out
of every kindred, and tongue, and people, and nation" (v.9).

Universal Worship (vv.11-14): Myriads of angels join: "Worthy is the Lamb
that was slain to receive power, and riches, and wisdom, and strength, and
honour, and glory, and blessing" (v.12). Every creature in all creation
worships (v.13). The elders fall down (v.14).


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/revelation-5/
 Guzik covers the sealed scroll, the Lion/Lamb paradox, the new song, and
 the expanding circles of worship.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "Worthy Is the Lamb" on v.12 (MTP Vol. 10, No. 561)
 "Worthy is the Lamb THAT WAS SLAIN — not despite being slain but BECAUSE
 He was slain. His worthiness is in His sacrifice. He conquered by dying.
 He earned all authority by giving up all authority. Seven things He is
 worthy to receive: power, riches, wisdom, strength, honour, glory,
 blessing. All of them — because He was slain. The cross is not His shame
 — it is His crown."
 Sermon: "The Lion and the Lamb" on vv.5-6 (MTP Vol. 20, No. 1196)
 "John hears 'the Lion' but sees 'a Lamb.' This is the great paradox of
 Christ: He conquers as a Lion BY being a slain Lamb. His power is in His
 sacrifice. His victory is through His death. He doesn't overcome by force
 but by blood. The Lion's roar is the Lamb's bleat. The throne is won by
 the cross."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1               | KJV         | "book" — Greek "biblion" — a scroll. ESV correctly renders this as "scroll" (the codex/book form didn't exist yet). Written on both sides (unusual — indicating fullness of content). Sealed with seven seals — completely sealed, requiring complete authority to open.
v.1               | ESV         | "scroll" — Greek "biblion" — a scroll. ESV correctly renders this as "scroll" (the codex/book form didn't exist yet). Written on both sides (unusual — indicating fullness of content). Sealed with seven seals — completely sealed, requiring complete authority to open.
v.5               | KJV         | "prevailed" — Greek "enikēsen" — conquered, overcame. ESV's "conquered" is more precise. The Lion conquered — but His method of conquest was the cross (v.6).
v.5               | ESV         | "conquered" — Greek "enikēsen" — conquered, overcame. ESV's "conquered" is more precise. The Lion conquered — but His method of conquest was the cross (v.6).
v.6               | KJV         | "as it had been slain" — Greek "hōs esphagmenon" — as having been slaughtered. Both equivalent in meaning. The Lamb bears the marks of slaughter — even in glory, the wounds remain. "Seven horns" = complete power. "Seven eyes" = complete knowledge/the fullness of the Spirit.
v.6               | ESV         | "as though it had been slain" — Greek "hōs esphagmenon" — as having been slaughtered. Both equivalent in meaning. The Lamb bears the marks of slaughter — even in glory, the wounds remain. "Seven horns" = complete power. "Seven eyes" = complete knowledge/the fullness of the Spirit.
v.8               | KJV         | "odours, which are the prayers of saints" — Greek "thumiamata" — incense. ESV translates directly; KJV uses "odours" (fragrances). The prayers of God's people are precious — stored in golden bowls, presented before the throne.
v.8               | ESV         | "incense, which are the prayers of the saints" — Greek "thumiamata" — incense. ESV translates directly; KJV uses "odours" (fragrances). The prayers of God's people are precious — stored in golden bowls, presented before the throne.
v.9               | KJV         | "a new song" — Greek "ōdēn kainēn" — a song never sung before. New because redemption is new — unprecedented, unrepeatable.
v.9               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "ōdēn kainēn" — a song never sung before. New because redemption is new — unprecedented, unrepeatable.
v.9               | KJV         | "every kindred, and tongue, and people, and nation" — KJV "kindred" = ESV "tribe" (Greek "phulēs"); KJV "tongue" = ESV "language" (Greek "glōssēs"). Both equivalent; ESV uses modern vocabulary. Four terms for universality. Redemption spans every ethnic, linguistic, cultural, and political boundary.
v.9               | ESV         | "every tribe and language and people and nation" — KJV "kindred" = ESV "tribe" (Greek "phulēs"); KJV "tongue" = ESV "language" (Greek "glōssēs"). Both equivalent; ESV uses modern vocabulary. Four terms for universality. Redemption spans every ethnic, linguistic, cultural, and political boundary.
v.12              | KJV         | "power, and riches, and wisdom, and strength, and honour, and glory, and blessing" — KJV "riches" = ESV "wealth" (Greek "ploutos"); KJV "strength" = ESV "might" (Greek "ischus"). Seven attributes: power (dunamis), riches/wealth (ploutos), wisdom (sophia), strength/might (ischus), honour/honor (timē), glory (doxa), blessing (eulogia). Complete, perfect praise.
v.12              | ESV         | "power and wealth and wisdom and might and honor and glory and blessing" — KJV "riches" = ESV "wealth" (Greek "ploutos"); KJV "strength" = ESV "might" (Greek "ischus"). Seven attributes: power (dunamis), riches/wealth (ploutos), wisdom (sophia), strength/might (ischus), honour/honor (timē), glory (doxa), blessing (eulogia). Complete, perfect praise.
v.5               | NET         | "the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the root of David, has conquered" — "Lion" and "root" — messianic titles from Genesis 49:9 and Isaiah 11:1/10. WEB: "the Lion who is of the tribe of Judah." But when John LOOKS, he sees a LAMB (v.6) — the Lion conquers as a Lamb.
v.6               | NET         | "a Lamb standing as though it had been killed" — "as though killed" (hOs esphagmenon = as having been slaughtered). WEB: "a Lamb standing, as though it had been slain." KJV: "a Lamb as it had been slain." The Lamb bears permanent marks of slaughter yet stands alive — resurrection body with crucifixion scars.
v.9               | NET         | "You are worthy to take the scroll and to open its seals because you were killed, and at the cost of your own blood you have purchased for God persons from every tribe" — "purchased" (Egorasas = bought in the marketplace). WEB: "you bought us for God with your blood." Redemption as commercial transaction — the Lamb's blood as purchase price.
v.12              | NET         | "Worthy is the Lamb who was killed to receive power and wealth and wisdom and might and honor and glory and praise!" — seven attributes (power, wealth, wisdom, might, honor, glory, praise). WEB: "Worthy is the Lamb who has been killed." Seven = completeness. The Lamb deserves ALL.
v.13              | NET         | "To the one seated on the throne and to the Lamb be praise and honor and glory and ruling power forever" — "to the one on the throne AND to the Lamb" — equal worship to God and Christ. WEB: "To him who sits on the throne, and to the Lamb." Co-worship = co-deity.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Sealed scroll     | God's plan for history and judgment. Completely sealed —                                                           | 
Lion of Judah     | Christ as the conquering King from David's line. Powerful,                                                         | 
Lamb as slain     | Christ as the sacrificed Lamb. Still bearing the marks of                                                          | 
Seven horns       | Complete, perfect power. Horns symbolize strength/authority                                                        | 
Seven eyes        | Complete knowledge and the fullness of the Spirit. All-                                                            | 
New song          | A song of redemption never sung before. Celebrating what                                                           | 
Prayers of saints | The prayers of God's people, stored as incense in golden                                                           | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "No man was found worthy" (v.3). Apart from Christ, no one can
   accomplish God's purposes. Are you trying to be your own savior, your
   own solution? Only the Lamb is worthy.

2. "A Lamb as it had been slain" (v.6). Christ conquers through sacrifice,
   not force. How does this "upside-down" power change your understanding
   of strength and victory?

3. "Redeemed us to God by thy blood out of every kindred, and tongue"
   (v.9). Heaven is multi-ethnic, multi-cultural, multi-lingual. Does your
   life and your church reflect this diversity?

4. "Worthy is the Lamb" (v.12). Is Christ worthy of your complete devotion
   — all seven attributes: your power, riches, wisdom, strength, honor,
   glory, and blessing? Are you giving Him all of it?

5. "The prayers of saints" (v.8). Your prayers are stored in golden bowls
   before God's throne. They are precious to Him. Does this motivate you to
   pray more?

6.notes:
Revelation - Chapter 6
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The Lamb opens six seals: white horse (conquest), red horse (war), black horse (famine), pale horse (death), martyrs crying "How long?", and cosmic upheaval — as the great day of wrath arrives and men cry to the mountains to hide them.


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: The Apostle John (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 6 begins the opening of the seven seals by the
Lamb. The first four seals release the famous "Four Horsemen of the
Apocalypse" — conquest, war, famine, and death. The fifth seal reveals
martyrs under the altar. The sixth seal brings cosmic upheaval. These
judgments represent God's wrath being poured out on a rebellious world. The
chapter ends with the terrified cry: "Who shall be able to stand?" (v.17).

The Four Horsemen (vv.1-8):
- First seal — White horse: conquest/deception (v.2). Debated: (1) the
 Antichrist as a false conqueror, or (2) the gospel going forth. Most see
 this as the Antichrist — a counterfeit of Christ (who rides a white horse
 in 19:11).
- Second seal — Red horse: war, peace removed from earth (vv.3-4).
- Third seal — Black horse: famine, economic collapse (vv.5-6). A day's
 wages for a day's food.
- Fourth seal — Pale/green horse: Death, with Hades following. Authority over
 a fourth of the earth (vv.7-8).

The Fifth Seal — Martyrs (vv.9-11): Souls under the altar — slain for God's
word and their testimony (v.9). They cry: "How long?" (v.10). Given white
robes; told to wait until the full number of martyrs is complete (v.11).

The Sixth Seal — Cosmic Upheaval (vv.12-17): Great earthquake, sun black,
moon blood-red, stars falling, sky rolled up, mountains moved (vv.12-14).
All people — from kings to slaves — hide in terror (vv.15-16). "The great day
of his wrath is come; and who shall be able to stand?" (v.17).


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/revelation-6/
 Guzik covers each seal, the four horsemen, the martyrs' cry, and the
 cosmic upheaval of the sixth seal.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "Who Shall Stand?" on v.17 (MTP Vol. 11, No. 650)
 "Who shall be able to stand? The answer: no one — in themselves. Kings
 cannot stand. Generals cannot stand. The rich cannot stand. The mighty
 cannot stand. When the Lamb's wrath is revealed, all human power is
 nothing. The only ones who can stand are those who stand IN the Lamb —
 covered by His blood, clothed in His righteousness. Apart from Him, no one
 stands."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2               | KJV         | "white horse" — White suggests victory/conquest. The rider has a bow (weapon) and a crown (stephanos — victor's wreath, not diadēma — royal crown). He goes forth "conquering and to conquer" — aggressive expansion.
v.2               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — White suggests victory/conquest. The rider has a bow (weapon) and a crown (stephanos — victor's wreath, not diadēma — royal crown). He goes forth "conquering and to conquer" — aggressive expansion.
v.6               | KJV         | "a measure of wheat for a penny" — Greek "dēnarion" — a day's wage. KJV's "penny" is misleading in modern English; ESV's "denarius" preserves the Roman coin name. KJV "measure" = ESV "quart" (Greek "choinix" — about a quart). A full day's work buys only enough food for one person. Severe economic hardship. "Hurt not the oil and the wine" — luxury items preserved while basics become unaffordable.
v.6               | ESV         | "a quart of wheat for a denarius" — Greek "dēnarion" — a day's wage. KJV's "penny" is misleading in modern English; ESV's "denarius" preserves the Roman coin name. KJV "measure" = ESV "quart" (Greek "choinix" — about a quart). A full day's work buys only enough food for one person. Severe economic hardship. "Hurt not the oil and the wine" — luxury items preserved while basics become unaffordable.
v.8               | KJV         | "pale horse" — Greek "chlōros" — green/pale (root of "chlorophyll"). The color of a corpse. Death personified rides this horse with Hades (the grave) following to collect the dead.
v.8               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "chlōros" — green/pale (root of "chlorophyll"). The color of a corpse. Death personified rides this horse with Hades (the grave) following to collect the dead.
v.9               | KJV         | "under the altar" — In the OT, the blood of sacrifices was poured at the base of the altar (Lev 4:7). Martyrs' lives were poured out as sacrifices — their souls are "under the altar."
v.9               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — In the OT, the blood of sacrifices was poured at the base of the altar (Lev 4:7). Martyrs' lives were poured out as sacrifices — their souls are "under the altar."
v.10              | KJV         | "How long, O Lord, holy and true, dost thou not judge and avenge our blood?" — KJV "Lord" = ESV "Sovereign Lord" (Greek "despotēs" — absolute ruler, not "kurios"). ESV captures the stronger Greek word. The cry of the suffering righteous throughout history (Psalm 13:1, 79:5, 94:3). Not revenge but justice — "holy and true" God must vindicate His people.
v.10              | ESV         | "O Sovereign Lord, holy and true, how long before you will judge and avenge our blood?" — KJV "Lord" = ESV "Sovereign Lord" (Greek "despotēs" — absolute ruler, not "kurios"). ESV captures the stronger Greek word. The cry of the suffering righteous throughout history (Psalm 13:1, 79:5, 94:3). Not revenge but justice — "holy and true" God must vindicate His people.
v.12              | KJV         | "black as sackcloth of hair" — Both equivalent. Sun darkened like black mourning cloth; KJV specifies "of hair" (goat-hair sackcloth). KJV: "moon became as blood" / ESV: "the full moon became like blood" — ESV adds "full" for clarity. Cosmic signs of divine judgment.
v.12              | ESV         | "black as sackcloth" — Both equivalent. Sun darkened like black mourning cloth; KJV specifies "of hair" (goat-hair sackcloth). KJV: "moon became as blood" / ESV: "the full moon became like blood" — ESV adds "full" for clarity. Cosmic signs of divine judgment.
v.16              | KJV         | "wrath of the Lamb" — A paradox: lambs are gentle, not wrathful. But this Lamb is also the Lion. His wrath is terrifying precisely because it comes from the one who offered mercy first.
v.16              | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — A paradox: lambs are gentle, not wrathful. But this Lamb is also the Lion. His wrath is terrifying precisely because it comes from the one who offered mercy first.
v.2               | NET         | "a white horse, and the one seated on it had a bow" — "white horse" (hippos leukos). WEB: "a white horse." KJV: "a white horse." Debated: Christ? Antichrist? Conquest in general? The crown given (not inherent) and the bow suggest a counterfeit conqueror.
v.9               | NET         | "the souls of those who had been violently killed because of the word of God" — "violently killed" (esphagmenOn = slaughtered — same word used of the Lamb in 5:6). WEB: "the souls of those who had been killed." Martyrs share the Lamb's experience of slaughter.
v.10              | NET         | "How long, Sovereign Master, holy and true, before you judge those who live on the earth and avenge our blood?" — "Sovereign Master" (ho despotEs = absolute ruler). WEB: "How long, Master, the holy and true." KJV: "How long, O Lord, holy and true." The martyrs' prayer for justice — not revenge but vindication.
v.12              | NET         | "the full moon became blood red" — "blood red" (hOs haima = as blood). WEB: "the whole moon became as blood." KJV: "the moon became as blood." Cosmic collapse at the sixth seal — sun dark, moon blood, stars falling, sky rolled up.
v.17              | NET         | "the great day of their wrath has come, and who is able to withstand it?" — "their wrath" (tEs orgEs autOn = the wrath of BOTH the Father and the Lamb). WEB: "the great day of his wrath has come." Some manuscripts: "his" (singular); others: "their" (plural). The Lamb has wrath — a terrifying paradox.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Four Horsemen     | Four riders representing conquest/deception, war, famine,                                                          | 
White horse (seal 1)| Conquest or deception. Likely the Antichrist as a                                                                  | 
Red horse         | War. Peace removed from the earth. Humanity destroying                                                             | 
Black horse       | Famine and economic collapse. A day's wages for a day's                                                            | 
Pale/green horse  | Death itself, with Hades following. Authority over a                                                               | 
Souls under the altar| Martyrs who died for their faith. Their blood cries                                                                | 
Wrath of the Lamb | Christ's righteous anger against sin and rebellion.                                                                | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. The four horsemen represent conquest, war, famine, and death. These are
   realities in our world today. How do you respond to global suffering —
   with despair, indifference, or faith that God is sovereign?

2. "How long, O Lord?" (v.10). Have you ever cried "how long?" — waiting
   for God's justice, waiting for relief, waiting for answers? The martyrs
   were told to wait. Can you?

3. "The wrath of the Lamb" (v.16). Christ is both Lamb (mercy) and Lion
   (judgment). Have you received His mercy? Those who reject the Lamb's
   mercy will face the Lamb's wrath.

4. "Who shall be able to stand?" (v.17). Can you stand before God? Not in
   your own righteousness — but in Christ's. Are you clothed in His
   righteousness or standing in your own?

5. These judgments are severe — but they come from a holy God responding to
   persistent rebellion. How does this chapter shape your urgency about
   sharing the gospel?

7.ESV:
1. After this I saw four angels standing at the four corners of the earth, holding back the four winds of the earth, that no wind might blow on earth or sea or against any tree.
2. Then I saw another angel ascending from the rising of the sun, with the seal of the living God, and he called with a loud voice to the four angels who had been given power to harm earth and sea,
3. saying, “Do not harm the earth or the sea or the trees, until we have sealed the servants of our God on their foreheads.”
4. And I heard the number of the sealed, 144,000, sealed from every tribe of the sons of Israel:
5. 12,000 from the tribe of Judah were sealed, 12,000 from the tribe of Reuben, 12,000 from the tribe of Gad,
6. 12,000 from the tribe of Asher, 12,000 from the tribe of Naphtali, 12,000 from the tribe of Manasseh,
7. 12,000 from the tribe of Simeon, 12,000 from the tribe of Levi, 12,000 from the tribe of Issachar,
8. 12,000 from the tribe of Zebulun, 12,000 from the tribe of Joseph, 12,000 from the tribe of Benjamin were sealed.
9. After this I looked, and behold, a great multitude that no one could number, from every nation, from all tribes and peoples and languages, standing before the throne and before the Lamb, clothed in white robes, with palm branches in their hands,
10. and crying out with a loud voice, “Salvation belongs to our God who sits on the throne, and to the Lamb!”
11. And all the angels were standing around the throne and around the elders and the four living creatures, and they fell on their faces before the throne and worshiped God,
12. saying, “Amen! Blessing and glory and wisdom and thanksgiving and honor and power and might be to our God forever and ever! Amen.”
13. Then one of the elders addressed me, saying, “Who are these, clothed in white robes, and from where have they come?”
14. I said to him, “Sir, you know.” And he said to me, “These are the ones coming out of the great tribulation. They have washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb.
15. “Therefore they are before the throne of God, and serve him day and night in his temple; and he who sits on the throne will shelter them with his presence.

7.notes:
Revelation - Chapter 7
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
An interlude: 144,000 from Israel are sealed for protection, and a great multitude from every nation stands before the throne in white robes — "these are they which came out of great tribulation, and have washed their robes in the blood of the Lamb."


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: The Apostle John (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 7 is an interlude between the sixth and seventh
seals — answering the question of 6:17: "Who shall be able to stand?" Answer:
those sealed by God. Two groups are presented: 144,000 sealed from Israel
(vv.1-8) and a great innumerable multitude from all nations (vv.9-17). This
chapter provides assurance: even during judgment, God protects His own.

The 144,000 Sealed (vv.1-8): Four angels hold back destructive winds (v.1).
Another angel with God's seal commands: don't harm the earth until God's
servants are sealed (vv.2-3). 144,000 sealed — 12,000 from each of 12 tribes
of Israel (vv.4-8). Interpretations: (1) literal Jewish believers preserved
during the tribulation, (2) symbolic of the complete church (spiritual Israel),
(3) Jewish evangelists during the tribulation.

The Great Multitude (vv.9-17): An innumerable multitude from every nation,
in white robes with palm branches (v.9). They cry: "Salvation to our God...
and unto the Lamb" (v.10). Angels, elders, and creatures worship (vv.11-12).
Identity: "These are they which came out of great tribulation, and have washed
their robes... in the blood of the Lamb" (v.14). Their reward: God's presence,
no more hunger/thirst/heat, the Lamb as shepherd, tears wiped away (vv.15-17).


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/revelation-7/
 Guzik covers the sealing of the 144,000, the identity debate, the great
 multitude, and the promises of eternal comfort.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "God Shall Wipe Away All Tears" on v.17 (MTP Vol. 15, No. 886)
 "God shall wipe away ALL tears — not some, all. Every tear of grief, of
 loss, of pain, of loneliness, of injustice — wiped away. And wiped by GOD
 HIMSELF — not an angel, not a servant — God's own hand on your face,
 removing every trace of sorrow. This is the tenderness of the Almighty —
 the God who made galaxies stoops to wipe tears from His children's eyes."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.3               | KJV         | "sealed" — Greek "sphragisōmen" — to stamp with a seal. Marks ownership and protection. God's seal = divine protection during judgment.
v.3               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "sphragisōmen" — to stamp with a seal. Marks ownership and protection. God's seal = divine protection during judgment.
v.4               | KJV         | "an hundred and forty and four thousand" — Both equivalent; ESV uses numerals, KJV spells out. 12 x 12 x 1,000. Whether literal or symbolic, it represents completeness — not one of God's chosen is missing.
v.4               | ESV         | "144,000" — Both equivalent; ESV uses numerals, KJV spells out. 12 x 12 x 1,000. Whether literal or symbolic, it represents completeness — not one of God's chosen is missing.
v.9               | KJV         | "a great multitude, which no man could number" — In contrast to the specific 144,000, this group is innumerable. The full harvest of redemption from all peoples.
v.9               | ESV         | "great multitude that no one could number" — In contrast to the specific 144,000, this group is innumerable. The full harvest of redemption from all peoples.
v.14              | KJV         | "great tribulation" — Greek "tēs thlipseōs tēs megalēs" — THE tribulation, THE great one. The specific end-times period of unprecedented suffering (cf. Matt 24:21, Dan 12:1).
v.14              | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "tēs thlipseōs tēs megalēs" — THE tribulation, THE great one. The specific end-times period of unprecedented suffering (cf. Matt 24:21, Dan 12:1).
v.14              | KJV         | "washed their robes... in the blood of the Lamb" — A paradox: blood that whitens. Christ's blood cleanses rather than stains.
v.14              | ESV         | "washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb" — A paradox: blood that whitens. Christ's blood cleanses rather than stains.
v.15              | KJV         | "shall dwell among them" — Greek "skēnosei ep' autous" — will spread His tabernacle over them. ESV's "shelter with his presence" captures the protective tent/tabernacle imagery better than KJV's "dwell among."
v.15              | ESV         | "will shelter them with his presence" — Greek "skēnosei ep' autous" — will spread His tabernacle over them. ESV's "shelter with his presence" captures the protective tent/tabernacle imagery better than KJV's "dwell among."
v.17              | KJV         | "living fountains of waters" — Greek "zōsas pēgas hudatōn" — springs of living water. KJV "fountains" vs. ESV "springs" — same Greek word "pēgas." ESV's word order follows the Greek more naturally. Eternal refreshment, never- ending satisfaction (cf. John 4:14, 7:38).
v.17              | ESV         | "springs of living water" — Greek "zōsas pēgas hudatōn" — springs of living water. KJV "fountains" vs. ESV "springs" — same Greek word "pēgas." ESV's word order follows the Greek more naturally. Eternal refreshment, never- ending satisfaction (cf. John 4:14, 7:38).
v.3               | NET         | "Do not damage the earth or the sea or the trees until we have put a seal on the foreheads of the servants of our God" — "seal" (sphragisOmen = stamp with a mark of ownership/protection). WEB: "until we have sealed the bondservants of our God." Divine marking before judgment falls — protection for God's own.
v.9               | NET         | "a large crowd that no one could count, from every nation, tribe, people, and language" — "no one could count" (arithmEsai auton oudeis edynato). WEB: "a great multitude, which no man could count." The redeemed are innumerable — God's saving purpose succeeds on a massive scale.
v.14              | NET         | "These are the ones who have come out of the great tribulation. They have washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb" — "washed... in blood" — paradox: blood that whitens. WEB: "washed their robes, and made them white in the Lamb's blood." Purity through sacrifice, not self-effort.
v.16              | NET         | "They will never go hungry or be thirsty again, and the sun will not beat down on them" — echoing Isaiah 49:10. WEB: "They will never be hungry or thirsty any more." The end of all deprivation — reversing the sufferings of earthly tribulation.
v.17              | NET         | "the Lamb in the middle of the throne will shepherd them and lead them to springs of living water" — "shepherd" (poimanei = tend/feed/guide). WEB: "the Lamb who is in the middle of the throne will be their shepherd." The slain Lamb becomes the Shepherd — imagery from Psalm 23 and John 10 consummated in eternity.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Sealed in foreheads| Marked by God for protection. Divine ownership and                                                                 | 
Great multitude   | The innumerable company of the redeemed from all nations.                                                          | 
Great tribulation | The unprecedented period of suffering before Christ's                                                              | 
Washed in the blood| Cleansed by Christ's sacrifice. Made pure through His                                                              | 
God shall wipe away tears| The ultimate comfort: God personally removing                                                                      | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Sealed the servants of our God" (v.3). God protects His own even in
   judgment. Are you sealed — marked as His? Do you live with the
   confidence of divine protection?

2. "A great multitude, which no man could number" (v.9). Heaven will be
   vastly diverse — every nation, language, people. Does your life and
   witness contribute to this multitude?

3. "Came out of great tribulation" (v.14). These saints endured the worst
   and came through. Whatever you're facing, you can come through it too —
   washed in the Lamb's blood.

4. "God shall wipe away all tears" (v.17). Every sorrow has an expiration
   date. God Himself will personally remove every tear. Does this hope
   sustain you in current grief?

5. "The Lamb... shall feed them, and shall lead them" (v.17). The Lamb is
   your shepherd — feeding, leading, satisfying. Are you following Him to
   living waters?

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Revelation Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/revelation/

David Guzik — Revelation 7 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/revelation-7/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================

Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

8.ESV:
1. When the Lamb opened the seventh seal, there was silence in heaven for about half an hour.
2. Then I saw the seven angels who stand before God, and seven trumpets were given to them.
3. And another angel came and stood at the altar with a golden censer, and he was given much incense to offer with the prayers of all the saints on the golden altar before the throne,
4. and the smoke of the incense, with the prayers of the saints, rose before God from the hand of the angel.
5. Then the angel took the censer and filled it with fire from the altar and threw it on the earth, and there were peals of thunder, rumblings, flashes of lightning, and an earthquake.
6. Now the seven angels who had the seven trumpets prepared to blow them.
7. The first angel blew his trumpet, and there followed hail and fire, mixed with blood, and these were thrown upon the earth. And a third of the earth was burned up, and a third of the trees were burned up, and all green grass was burned up.
8. The second angel blew his trumpet, and something like a great mountain, burning with fire, was thrown into the sea, and a third of the sea became blood.
9. A third of the living creatures in the sea died, and a third of the ships were destroyed.
10. The third angel blew his trumpet, and a great star fell from heaven, blazing like a torch, and it fell on a third of the rivers and on the springs of water.
11. The name of the star is Wormwood. A third of the waters became wormwood, and many people died from the water, because it had been made bitter.
12. The fourth angel blew his trumpet, and a third of the sun was struck, and a third of the moon, and a third of the stars, so that a third of their light might be darkened, and a third of the day might be kept from shining, and likewise a third of the night.
13. Then I looked, and I heard an eagle crying with a loud voice as it flew directly overhead, “Woe, woe, woe to those who dwell on the earth, at the blasts of the other trumpets that the three angels are about to blow!”

8.notes:
Revelation - Chapter 8
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The seventh seal brings silence in heaven for half an hour, then seven angels with trumpets begin to sound — hail and fire, a burning mountain, a falling star, and darkness striking a third of the earth, sea, rivers, and sky.


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: The Apostle John (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 8 opens the seventh seal — which contains the
seven trumpet judgments (a pattern of judgments within judgments). The seventh
seal begins with dramatic silence in heaven (v.1), then the prayers of saints
are offered (vv.3-4), and the first four trumpets sound (vv.7-12). These
trumpet judgments intensify the seal judgments — affecting a third of the
earth rather than a quarter (cf. 6:8).

The Seventh Seal — Silence (v.1): Half an hour of silence in heaven. After
the ceaseless worship of chapters 4-5, silence is stunning. It represents
awe, anticipation, and the solemnity of what's coming.

Prayers and Fire (vv.2-6): Seven angels receive seven trumpets (v.2). Another
angel offers incense with the prayers of saints (vv.3-4). Then the censer is
filled with fire and cast to earth — voices, thunder, lightning, earthquake
(v.5). The prayers of saints trigger judgment.

The First Four Trumpets (vv.7-12):
- Trumpet 1 (v.7): Hail and fire mixed with blood. A third of trees and all
 green grass burned. (Echoes the Egyptian plague of hail, Exodus 9:23-25.)
- Trumpet 2 (vv.8-9): A great burning mountain cast into the sea. A third of
 the sea becomes blood, a third of sea creatures die, a third of ships
 destroyed.
- Trumpet 3 (vv.10-11): A star called Wormwood falls on rivers/springs. A
 third of waters become bitter; many die.
- Trumpet 4 (v.12): A third of sun, moon, and stars darkened. A third of
 daylight and nightlight removed.

Three Woes Announced (v.13): An angel (or eagle) flies through heaven
declaring three "woes" for the remaining three trumpets — the worst is yet
to come.


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/revelation-8/
 Guzik covers the silence, the prayers/fire connection, each trumpet
 judgment, and the three woes announcement.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "The Prayers of the Saints" on vv.3-4 (MTP Vol. 18, No. 1068)
 "The prayers of the saints — offered with incense, ascending before God.
 Your prayers are not lost. They are not forgotten. They rise to God's
 throne, mixed with the fragrance of Christ's intercession. And notice what
 follows: the censer filled with fire is cast to earth. Your prayers trigger
 God's action. Prayer moves the hand that moves the world."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1               | KJV         | "silence... about the space of half an hour" — Greek "sigē" — silence, stillness. After the constant worship of heaven, silence is dramatic. It signals the gravity of what's about to happen.
v.1               | ESV         | "silence in heaven for about half an hour" — Greek "sigē" — silence, stillness. After the constant worship of heaven, silence is dramatic. It signals the gravity of what's about to happen.
v.3               | KJV         | "incense" — Greek "thumiamata" — incense offerings. The prayers of saints are mixed with incense (Christ's intercession) and presented to God. Prayer is worship.
v.3               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "thumiamata" — incense offerings. The prayers of saints are mixed with incense (Christ's intercession) and presented to God. Prayer is worship.
v.7               | KJV         | "the third part of trees was burnt up" — ESV follows a textual variant that includes "a third of the earth" (found in earlier manuscripts). A recurring fraction in the trumpet judgments. Not total destruction (yet) but severe, escalating judgment. One-third = significant but not complete.
v.7               | ESV         | "a third of the earth was burned up, and a third of the trees were burned up" — ESV follows a textual variant that includes "a third of the earth" (found in earlier manuscripts). A recurring fraction in the trumpet judgments. Not total destruction (yet) but severe, escalating judgment. One-third = significant but not complete.
v.11              | KJV         | "Wormwood" — Greek "Apsinthos" — a bitter plant. Symbolizes bitterness, poison, judgment. The star's name describes its effect: making waters deadly.
v.11              | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "Apsinthos" — a bitter plant. Symbolizes bitterness, poison, judgment. The star's name describes its effect: making waters deadly.
v.13              | KJV         | "angel" — Some manuscripts read "eagle" (aetou) instead of "angel" (angelou). ESV follows the earlier manuscript reading "eagle" — a bird of prey announcing doom. Either way, the message is clear: three woes are coming. KJV: "inhabiters of the earth" / ESV: "those who dwell on the earth" — Both equivalent; a technical term in Revelation for unbelievers.
v.13              | ESV         | "eagle" — Some manuscripts read "eagle" (aetou) instead of "angel" (angelou). ESV follows the earlier manuscript reading "eagle" — a bird of prey announcing doom. Either way, the message is clear: three woes are coming. KJV: "inhabiters of the earth" / ESV: "those who dwell on the earth" — Both equivalent; a technical term in Revelation for unbelievers.
v.1               | NET         | "Now when the Lamb opened the seventh seal there was silence in heaven for about half an hour" — "silence" (sigE = hush). WEB: "there was silence in heaven for about half an hour." The only silence in heaven in all of Revelation — dramatic pause before the final judgments. Even angels hold their breath.
v.3               | NET         | "he was given a large amount of incense to offer up, with the prayers of all the saints" — "with the prayers" (tais proseuchais tOn hagiOn). WEB: "with the prayers of all the saints." Prayers of the saints rise as incense to God's throne — they are heard and they trigger action (v.5).
v.5               | NET         | "the angel took the censer, filled it with fire from the altar, and threw it on the earth" — prayers answered with fire — the saints' cries for justice (6:10) now produce thunders, flashes, earthquake. WEB: "threw it to the earth." Prayer produces cosmic consequences.
v.11              | NET         | "The name of the star is Wormwood" — "Wormwood" (ho Apsinthos = bitter herb/absinthe). WEB: "The name of the star is called Wormwood." KJV: "the name of the star is called Wormwood." Bitterness poisons the waters — a third of rivers made deadly.
v.13              | NET         | "Woe! Woe! Woe to those who live on the earth" — triple "woe" (ouai ouai ouai) — one for each remaining trumpet. WEB: "Woe! Woe! Woe to those who dwell on the earth." The worst is yet to come — the first four trumpets are preliminary to the final three.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Silence in heaven | A dramatic pause before the trumpet judgments. Awe,                                                                | 
Prayers of saints | Believers' prayers ascending to God, mixed with incense.                                                           | 
Trumpets          | Seven judgments of increasing severity. Trumpets announce                                                          | 
One-third         | The fraction affected by trumpet judgments. Severe but not                                                         | 
Wormwood          | Bitterness and poison. A star that contaminates earth's                                                            | 
Three woes        | The final three trumpet judgments (5th, 6th, 7th). The                                                             | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Silence in heaven" (v.1). Even heaven pauses before God's judgment. Do
   you take God's judgment seriously? Or do you treat it casually?

2. "The prayers of the saints" (vv.3-4). Your prayers reach God's throne
   and trigger His action. Do you pray like your prayers matter? They do —
   they ascend before God Himself.

3. The trumpet judgments echo the Egyptian plagues (hail, blood, darkness).
   God judged Egypt to deliver Israel; He judges the world to deliver His
   people. How does this pattern encourage you?

4. "The rest of the men... repented not" (9:20-21, previewing what
   follows). Even severe judgment doesn't automatically produce repentance.
   What does this say about the hardness of the human heart?

5. These judgments are severe but partial (one-third). God leaves room for
   repentance. Are you responding to God's warnings, or ignoring them?

9.notes:
Revelation - Chapter 9
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The fifth trumpet opens the bottomless pit releasing tormenting locusts, and the sixth trumpet unleashes four angels who kill a third of mankind — yet those who survive still refuse to repent of their idolatry and murders.


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: The Apostle John (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 9 contains the fifth and sixth trumpet judgments
— the first two "woes" announced in 8:13. These are the most terrifying
judgments yet: demonic locusts from the abyss (5th trumpet) and a demonic
army of 200 million that kills a third of humanity (6th trumpet). Despite
this unprecedented devastation, the chapter ends with humanity's stubborn
refusal to repent (vv.20-21).

Fifth Trumpet / First Woe (vv.1-12): A star (fallen angel) opens the
bottomless pit (v.1). Smoke darkens the sky (v.2). Demonic locusts emerge —
not to kill but to torment for five months (vv.3-5). Men seek death but
cannot find it (v.6). The locusts are described in terrifying detail: horse-
like, crowned, human-faced, lion-toothed, armored, with scorpion tails
(vv.7-10). Their king: Abaddon/Apollyon — "Destroyer" (v.11).

Sixth Trumpet / Second Woe (vv.13-21): A voice from the golden altar commands
the release of four angels bound at the Euphrates (vv.13-14). They were
prepared for this exact moment — to kill a third of mankind (v.15). An army
of 200 million with lion-headed horses breathing fire, smoke, and brimstone
(vv.16-19). A third of humanity killed (v.18).

Humanity's Response (vv.20-21): Despite all this, "the rest of the men...
repented not" (v.20). They continued worshipping demons and idols, and
refused to repent of murders, sorceries, fornication, and thefts (vv.20-21).


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/revelation-9/
 Guzik covers the abyss, the demonic locusts, Abaddon/Apollyon, the
 200-million army, and humanity's refusal to repent.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "Impenitence" on vv.20-21 (MTP Vol. 20, No. 1192)
 "They repented NOT. After all this — after a third of humanity killed,
 after demonic torment, after cosmic devastation — they still refused to
 repent. This is the hardness of the human heart. Judgment alone does not
 produce repentance. Only grace produces repentance. You can terrify a man
 into compliance but not into love. Only the goodness of God leads to
 repentance (Romans 2:4)."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1               | KJV         | "a star fall from heaven" — Greek "peptokata" — perfect tense: having fallen (already fallen). This is a fallen angel — likely Satan or a high-ranking demon. "Bottomless pit" — Greek "abussos" — the abyss, the deep. The prison of demons.
v.1               | ESV         | "star fallen from heaven" — Greek "peptokata" — perfect tense: having fallen (already fallen). This is a fallen angel — likely Satan or a high-ranking demon. "Bottomless pit" — Greek "abussos" — the abyss, the deep. The prison of demons.
v.5               | KJV         | "five months" — The natural lifespan of locusts (May-September). A limited, defined period of torment.
v.5               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — The natural lifespan of locusts (May-September). A limited, defined period of torment.
v.6               | KJV         | "death shall flee from them" — An unprecedented condition: the inability to die. Torment without the escape of death.
v.6               | ESV         | "death will flee from them" — An unprecedented condition: the inability to die. Torment without the escape of death.
v.11              | KJV         | "Abaddon... Apollyon" — Hebrew and Greek names both meaning "Destroyer." The king of the demonic locusts — a powerful fallen angel.
v.11              | ESV         | "Abaddon, and in Greek he is called Apollyon" — Hebrew and Greek names both meaning "Destroyer." The king of the demonic locusts — a powerful fallen angel.
v.15              | KJV         | "prepared for an hour, and a day, and a month, and a year" — God's precise timing. These angels were prepared for this exact moment in history. Nothing is random in God's plan.
v.15              | ESV         | "prepared for the hour, the day, the month, and the year" — God's precise timing. These angels were prepared for this exact moment in history. Nothing is random in God's plan.
v.16              | KJV         | "two hundred thousand thousand" — Both render the Greek "dismyriades myriadōn" (two myriads of myriads) = 200 million. KJV uses English multiplication; ESV follows the Greek phrasing more literally. Whether literal or symbolic of an incomprehensibly large force, it represents overwhelming destructive power.
v.16              | ESV         | "twice ten thousand times ten thousand" — Both render the Greek "dismyriades myriadōn" (two myriads of myriads) = 200 million. KJV uses English multiplication; ESV follows the Greek phrasing more literally. Whether literal or symbolic of an incomprehensibly large force, it represents overwhelming destructive power.
v.20              | KJV         | "repented not of the works of their hands" — Greek "ou metenoesan" — did not change their minds, did not turn. ESV adds "nor give up worshiping" for clarity. Despite unprecedented judgment, the human heart remains stubbornly resistant without grace.
v.20              | ESV         | "did not repent of the works of their hands nor give up worshiping demons and idols" — Greek "ou metenoesan" — did not change their minds, did not turn. ESV adds "nor give up worshiping" for clarity. Despite unprecedented judgment, the human heart remains stubbornly resistant without grace.
v.21              | KJV         | "sorceries" — Both equivalent. Greek "pharmakōn" — drugs, potions, sorcery. Root of English "pharmacy." Drug use connected with occult practices. KJV: "fornication" / ESV: "sexual immorality" — Greek "porneias." ESV uses the broader, more accurate modern term.
v.21              | ESV         | "sorceries" — Both equivalent. Greek "pharmakōn" — drugs, potions, sorcery. Root of English "pharmacy." Drug use connected with occult practices. KJV: "fornication" / ESV: "sexual immorality" — Greek "porneias." ESV uses the broader, more accurate modern term.
v.1               | NET         | "a star that had fallen from heaven to earth, and he was given the key to the shaft of the abyss" — "a star fallen" (astera peptOkota = a star having fallen — perfect participle). WEB: "a star from the sky which had fallen." Not a meteorite but a personal being (given a key) — likely a fallen angel.
v.3               | NET         | "locusts came out of the smoke onto the earth" — "locusts" (akrides) — but not ordinary locusts (v.4: they don't harm vegetation). WEB: "out of the smoke came locusts." Demonic beings in locust form — tormenting but not killing.
v.6               | NET         | "In those days people will seek death, but will not be able to find it; they will long to die, but death will flee from them" — "death will flee" (pheuxetai ho thanatos). WEB: "death will flee from them." A terrifying inversion — death itself becomes unattainable. Suffering without the escape of dying.
v.11              | NET         | "whose name in Hebrew is Abaddon, and in Greek, Apollyon" — both names mean "Destroyer." WEB: "In Hebrew, Abaddon, but in Greek, Apollyon." KJV: "Abaddon... Apollyon." Named in two languages for both audiences — the king over the abyss army.
v.20              | NET         | "The rest of humanity, who had not been killed by these plagues, did not repent" — "did not repent" (ou metenoEsan = refused to change their minds). WEB: "didn't repent." The purpose of judgment is repentance (cf. 16:9, 11) — but humanity refuses. Hardened beyond response.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Bottomless pit/Abyss| The prison of demons. A place of confinement for                                                                   | 
Demonic locusts   | Supernatural beings from the abyss. Not natural insects                                                            | 
Abaddon/Apollyon  | "Destroyer." The king of the abyss locusts. A powerful                                                             | 
Five months       | The limited duration of the locust torment. God sets                                                               | 
Repented not      | Humanity's stubborn refusal to turn to God despite                                                                 | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Men seek death, and shall not find it" (v.6). There are fates worse
   than death. This should produce urgency about the gospel — people need
   to be rescued before judgment falls.

2. "Repented not" (v.20). Judgment alone doesn't produce repentance — only
   grace does. Are you relying on God's grace for transformation, or trying
   to scare yourself into obedience?

3. God's timing is precise (v.15) — "an hour, and a day, and a month, and a
   year." Nothing in God's plan is random or accidental. Can you trust His
   timing in your life?

4. The sealed (7:3) are protected from the locusts (v.4). God distinguishes
   between His people and the world. Are you sealed — marked as His?

5. "Neither repented they of their murders, nor of their sorceries, nor of
   their fornication, nor of their thefts" (v.21). Four categories of sin
   that persist despite judgment. Which ones characterize our culture
   today? Are you standing against them?

10.notes:
Revelation - Chapter 10
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A mighty angel with a little scroll announces that when the seventh trumpet sounds, the mystery of God will be finished — John eats the scroll which is sweet in his mouth but bitter in his stomach, and is told to prophesy again.


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: The Apostle John (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 10 is an interlude between the sixth and seventh
trumpets (like chapter 7 was between the sixth and seventh seals). A mighty
angel descends with a little scroll, seven thunders speak (but are sealed),
and John is told to eat the scroll — sweet in the mouth but bitter in the
stomach. This chapter prepares for the final trumpet and the completion of
God's mystery.

The Mighty Angel (vv.1-4): A powerful angel descends — clothed with a cloud,
rainbow on his head, face like the sun, feet like fire (v.1). He straddles
sea and land (v.2) — claiming authority over all creation. He cries with a
lion's voice; seven thunders respond (v.3). John is told NOT to write what
the thunders said — some things remain sealed (v.4).

The Oath (vv.5-7): The angel swears by the eternal Creator: "there should be
time no longer" (v.6) — no more delay. When the seventh trumpet sounds, "the
mystery of God should be finished" (v.7).

The Little Scroll (vv.8-11): John is told to eat the scroll (v.8). It is
sweet as honey in his mouth but bitter in his stomach (vv.9-10). He must
"prophesy again" to many peoples and nations (v.11). God's word is sweet
(truth, promise) but bitter (judgment, suffering).


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/revelation-10/
 Guzik covers the mighty angel's identity, the sealed thunders, the oath
 of no more delay, and the sweet/bitter scroll.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "The Sweet and the Bitter" on vv.9-10 (MTP Vol. 25, No. 1502)
 "Sweet in the mouth — God's word is delightful to receive. Truth is sweet.
 Promise is sweet. The gospel is sweet. But bitter in the belly — when you
 digest it, when you live it out, when you proclaim judgment, when you see
 its implications for a lost world — it becomes bitter. The prophet who
 truly digests God's word will know both sweetness and sorrow."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1               | KJV         | "his feet as pillars of fire" — Greek "podes" can mean feet or legs; ESV's "legs" better fits "pillars." The angel's description echoes Christ (1:15-16) but is likely a powerful angel, not Christ Himself (he swears by God in v.6).
v.1               | ESV         | "his legs like pillars of fire" — Greek "podes" can mean feet or legs; ESV's "legs" better fits "pillars." The angel's description echoes Christ (1:15-16) but is likely a powerful angel, not Christ Himself (he swears by God in v.6).
v.4               | KJV         | "seal up" — Greek "sphragison" — seal, close up. Some revelation is withheld. Not everything is revealed — God has secrets.
v.4               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "sphragison" — seal, close up. Some revelation is withheld. Not everything is revealed — God has secrets.
v.6               | KJV         | "that there should be time no longer" — Greek "chronos ouketi estai" — ESV's "no more delay" is the better translation. Not that time ceases to exist but that God's delay in completing His plan is over. KJV's "time no longer" can be misread as the end of time itself.
v.6               | ESV         | "that there would be no more delay" — Greek "chronos ouketi estai" — ESV's "no more delay" is the better translation. Not that time ceases to exist but that God's delay in completing His plan is over. KJV's "time no longer" can be misread as the end of time itself.
v.7               | KJV         | "mystery of God" — Greek "mustērion tou theou" — God's hidden plan now brought to completion. The full outworking of His redemptive purpose.
v.7               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "mustērion tou theou" — God's hidden plan now brought to completion. The full outworking of His redemptive purpose.
v.9               | KJV         | "eat it up" — Greek "kataphage" — devour it, consume it completely. Both equivalent in sense. Like Ezekiel (3:1-3), John must internalize God's message before proclaiming it.
v.9               | ESV         | "take and eat it" — Greek "kataphage" — devour it, consume it completely. Both equivalent in sense. Like Ezekiel (3:1-3), John must internalize God's message before proclaiming it.
v.1               | NET         | "I saw another powerful angel descending from heaven, wrapped in a cloud, with a rainbow above his head" — "powerful angel" (angelon ischyron = a strong angel). WEB: "a mighty angel coming down out of the sky." Not Christ (angels are sent; Christ sends) but a glorious messenger.
v.6               | NET         | "there will be no more delay!" — "no more delay" (chronos ouketi estai = time will be no more/no more time). WEB: "there will no longer be delay." KJV: "there should be time no longer." Debated: no more delay before judgment? or time itself ceasing? Context favors: no further postponement.
v.7               | NET         | "the mystery of God is completed, just as he announced to his servants the prophets" — "mystery" (mystErion = hidden plan now revealed). WEB: "the mystery of God is finished." KJV: "the mystery of God should be finished." God's secret plan reaches its culmination.
v.9               | NET         | "Take the scroll and eat it. It will be bitter in your stomach, but sweet as honey in your mouth" — "eat it" (kataphage = devour/consume completely). WEB: "Take it, and eat it up." Echoing Ezekiel 3:1-3 — internalizing God's word. Sweet truth contains bitter content (judgment).
v.11              | NET         | "You must prophesy again about many peoples, nations, languages, and kings" — "must" (dei = divine necessity). WEB: "You must prophesy again." John's commission is not finished — more revelation follows. The universal scope continues.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Mighty angel      | A powerful angelic being with authority over sea and land.                                                         | 
Seven thunders    | A sealed revelation. God spoke but John was forbidden to                                                           | 
No more delay     | God's patience has a limit. His plan will reach completion                                                         | 
Mystery of God finished| God's hidden plan brought to full completion. What                                                                 | 
Sweet and bitter scroll| God's word: sweet to receive (truth, promise) but                                                                  | 
Prophesy again    | John's continued mission to proclaim God's word to all                                                             | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "There should be time no longer" (v.6). God's patience has a limit. Are
   you living with urgency, knowing that delay will end and God's plan will
   be completed?

2. "Seal up those things" (v.4). God has secrets — things He chooses not to
   reveal. Can you accept that some questions won't be answered this side
   of eternity? Can you trust what He hasn't revealed?

3. "Sweet as honey... belly was bitter" (vv.9-10). God's word is both sweet
   and bitter. Do you experience both? The sweetness of His promises AND
   the bitterness of His judgments? A balanced diet of Scripture includes
   both.

4. "Thou must prophesy again" (v.11). After receiving God's word, you must
   share it. Are you keeping truth to yourself or proclaiming it to others?

------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Revelation Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/revelation/

David Guzik — Revelation 10 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/revelation-10/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

11.ESV:
1. Then I was given a measuring rod like a staff, and I was told, “Rise and measure the temple of God and the altar and those who worship there,
2. but do not measure the court outside the temple; leave that out, for it is given over to the nations, and they will trample the holy city for forty-two months.
3. And I will grant authority to my two witnesses, and they will prophesy for 1,260 days, clothed in sackcloth.”
4. These are the two olive trees and the two lampstands that stand before the Lord of the earth.
5. And if anyone would harm them, fire pours from their mouth and consumes their foes. If anyone would harm them, this is how he is doomed to be killed.
6. They have the power to shut the sky, that no rain may fall during the days of their prophesying, and they have power over the waters to turn them into blood and to strike the earth with every kind of plague, as often as they desire.
7. And when they have finished their testimony, the beast that rises from the bottomless pit will make war on them and conquer them and kill them,
8. and their dead bodies will lie in the street of the great city that symbolically is called Sodom and Egypt, where their Lord was crucified.
9. For three and a half days some from the peoples and tribes and languages and nations will gaze at their dead bodies and refuse to let them be placed in a tomb,
10. and those who dwell on the earth will rejoice over them and make merry and exchange presents, because these two prophets had been a torment to those who dwell on the earth.
11. But after the three and a half days a breath of life from God entered them, and they stood up on their feet, and great fear fell on those who saw them.
12. Then they heard a loud voice from heaven saying to them, “Come up here!” And they went up to heaven in a cloud, and their enemies watched them.
13. And at that hour there was a great earthquake, and a tenth of the city fell. Seven thousand people were killed in the earthquake, and the rest were terrified and gave glory to the God of heaven.
14. The second woe has passed; behold, the third woe is soon to come.
15. Then the seventh angel blew his trumpet, and there were loud voices in heaven, saying, “The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ, and he shall reign forever and ever.”
16. And the twenty-four elders who sit on their thrones before God fell on their faces and worshiped God,
17. saying, “We give thanks to you, Lord God Almighty, who is and who was, for you have taken your great power and begun to reign.
18. The nations raged, but your wrath came, and the time for the dead to be judged, and for rewarding your servants, the prophets and saints, and those who fear your name, both small and great, and for destroying the destroyers of the earth.”
19. Then God’s temple in heaven was opened, and the ark of his covenant was seen within his temple. There were flashes of lightning, rumblings, peals of thunder, an earthquake, and heavy hail.
