1. Then a measuring rod like a staff was given to me, and I was told, “Get up and measure the temple of God, and the altar, and the ones who worship there.
2. But do not measure the outer courtyard of the temple; leave it out because it has been given to the Gentiles, and they will trample on the holy city for forty-two months.
3. And I will grant my two witnesses authority to prophesy for 1,260 days, dressed in sackcloth.”
4. (These are the two olive trees and the two lampstands that stand before the Lord of the earth.)
5. If anyone wants to harm them, fire comes out of their mouths and completely consumes their enemies. If anyone wants to harm them, they must be killed this way.
6. These two have the power to close up the sky so that it does not rain during the time they are prophesying. They have power to turn the waters to blood and to strike the earth with every kind of plague whenever they want.
7. When they have completed their testimony, the beast that comes up from the abyss will make war on them and conquer them and kill them.
8. Their corpses will lie in the street of the great city that is symbolically called Sodom and Egypt, where their Lord was also crucified.
9. For three and a half days those from every people, tribe, nation, and language will look at their corpses because they will not permit them to be placed in a tomb.
10. And those who live on the earth will rejoice over them and celebrate, even sending gifts to each other because these two prophets had tormented those who live on the earth.
11. But after three and a half days a breath of life from God entered them, and they stood on their feet, and tremendous fear seized those who were watching them.
12. Then they heard a loud voice from heaven saying to them: “Come up here!” So the two prophets went up to heaven in a cloud while their enemies stared at them.
13. Just then a major earthquake took place and a tenth of the city collapsed; seven thousand people were killed in the earthquake, and the rest were terrified and gave glory to the God of heaven.
14. The second woe has come and gone; the third is coming quickly.
15. Then the seventh angel blew his trumpet, and there were loud voices in heaven saying: “The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ, and he will reign for ever and ever.”
16. Then the twenty-four elders who are seated on their thrones before God threw themselves down with their faces to the ground and worshiped God
17. with these words: “We give you thanks, Lord God, the All-Powerful, the one who is and who was, because you have taken your great power and begun to reign.
18. The nations were enraged, but your wrath has come, and the time has come for the dead to be judged, and the time has come to give to your servants, the prophets, their reward, as well as to the saints and to those who revere your name, both small and great, and the time has come to destroy those who destroy the earth.”
19. Then the temple of God in heaven was opened, and the ark of his covenant was visible within his temple. And there were flashes of lightning, roaring, crashes of thunder, an earthquake, and a great hailstorm.
11.WEB:
1. A reed like a rod was given to me. Someone said, “Rise, and measure God’s temple, and the altar, and those who worship in it.
2. Leave out the court which is outside of the temple, and don’t measure it, for it has been given to the nations. They will tread the holy city under foot for forty-two months.
3. I will give power to my two witnesses, and they will prophesy one thousand two hundred sixty days, clothed in sackcloth.”
4. These are the two olive trees and the two lamp stands, standing before the Lord of the earth.
5. If anyone desires to harm them, fire proceeds out of their mouth and devours their enemies. If anyone desires to harm them, he must be killed in this way.
6. These have the power to shut up the sky, that it may not rain during the days of their prophecy. They have power over the waters, to turn them into blood, and to strike the earth with every plague, as often as they desire.
7. When they have finished their testimony, the beast that comes up out of the abyss will make war with them, and overcome them, and kill them.
8. Their dead bodies will be in the street of the great city, which spiritually is called Sodom and Egypt, where also their Lord was crucified.
9. From among the peoples, tribes, languages, and nations people will look at their dead bodies for three and a half days, and will not allow their dead bodies to be laid in a tomb.
10. Those who dwell on the earth rejoice over them, and they will be glad. They will give gifts to one another, because these two prophets tormented those who dwell on the earth.
11. After the three and a half days, the breath of life from God entered into them, and they stood on their feet. Great fear fell on those who saw them.
12. I heard a loud voice from heaven saying to them, “Come up here!” They went up into heaven in the cloud, and their enemies saw them.
13. In that day there was a great earthquake, and a tenth of the city fell. Seven thousand people were killed in the earthquake, and the rest were terrified, and gave glory to the God of heaven.
14. The second woe is past. Behold, the third woe comes quickly.
15. The seventh angel sounded, and great voices in heaven followed, saying, “The kingdom of the world has become the Kingdom of our Lord, and of his Christ. He will reign forever and ever!”
16. The twenty-four elders, who sit on their thrones before God’s throne, fell on their faces and worshiped God,
17. saying: “We give you thanks, Lord God, the Almighty, the one who is and who was; because you have taken your great power, and reigned.
18. The nations were angry, and your wrath came, as did the time for the dead to be judged, and to give your bondservants the prophets, their reward, as well as to the saints, and those who fear your name, to the small and the great; and to destroy those who destroy the earth.”
19. God’s temple that is in heaven was opened, and the ark of the Lord’s covenant was seen in his temple. Lightnings, sounds, thunders, an earthquake, and great hail followed.
11.notes:
Revelation - Chapter 11
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Two witnesses prophesy for 1,260 days, are killed by the beast, lie dead in the street for three days, then are resurrected and ascend to heaven — the seventh trumpet sounds: "The kingdoms of this world are become the kingdoms of our Lord, and of his Christ."


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: The Apostle John (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 11 continues the interlude with the measuring of
the temple (vv.1-2), the ministry of the two witnesses (vv.3-14), and then
the seventh trumpet sounds (vv.15-19). The two witnesses are among the most
debated figures in Revelation — powerful prophets who minister for 1,260 days,
are killed, resurrected, and taken to heaven. The seventh trumpet announces
Christ's kingdom.

The Temple Measured (vv.1-2): John measures the temple, altar, and worshippers
(v.1). The outer court is left unmeasured — given to Gentiles who trample
Jerusalem for 42 months (v.2). Measuring = claiming/protecting; not measuring
= giving over to judgment.

The Two Witnesses (vv.3-14): They prophesy 1,260 days in sackcloth (v.3).
Described as "two olive trees and two candlesticks" (v.4, cf. Zechariah 4).
They have power like Elijah (shutting rain) and Moses (turning water to blood)
(vv.5-6). After finishing their testimony, the beast kills them (v.7). Their
bodies lie in Jerusalem's streets for 3.5 days while the world celebrates
(vv.8-10). Then God resurrects them and calls them to heaven (vv.11-12). An
earthquake kills 7,000 (v.13).

The Seventh Trumpet (vv.15-19): "The kingdoms of this world are become the
kingdoms of our Lord, and of his Christ; and he shall reign for ever and ever"
(v.15). The elders worship (vv.16-17). God's wrath has come; time to judge
the dead and reward the faithful (v.18). The heavenly temple opens, revealing
the ark of the covenant (v.19).


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/revelation-11/
 Guzik covers the temple measurement, the two witnesses' identity and
 ministry, their death and resurrection, and the seventh trumpet.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "The Kingdoms of This World" on v.15 (MTP Vol. 20, No. 1199)
 "The kingdoms of this world ARE BECOME — not 'will become' but 'are become.'
 From heaven's perspective, it is already accomplished. Christ's reign is
 certain. Every earthly kingdom will bow. Every human authority will submit.
 He SHALL reign for ever and ever. This is not a hope — it is a certainty.
 The question is not IF but WHEN."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2               | KJV         | "forty and two months" — 3.5 years. The same period as 1,260 days (v.3) and "time, times, and half a time" (12:14). The duration of the tribulation's second half.
v.2               | ESV         | "forty-two months" — 3.5 years. The same period as 1,260 days (v.3) and "time, times, and half a time" (12:14). The duration of the tribulation's second half.
v.3               | KJV         | "two witnesses" — Identity debated: (1) Moses and Elijah (miracles match), (2) Enoch and Elijah (both taken without dying), (3) symbolic of the church's witness, (4) two literal end-times prophets. Their powers echo Moses (plagues) and Elijah (drought).
v.3               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Identity debated: (1) Moses and Elijah (miracles match), (2) Enoch and Elijah (both taken without dying), (3) symbolic of the church's witness, (4) two literal end-times prophets. Their powers echo Moses (plagues) and Elijah (drought).
v.4               | KJV         | "two olive trees, and the two candlesticks" — KJV "candlesticks" = ESV "lampstands" (Greek "luchniai" — same word as in 1:20). From Zechariah 4. Olive trees supply oil (the Spirit); lampstands give light (witness). Spirit-empowered testimony.
v.4               | ESV         | "two olive trees and the two lampstands" — KJV "candlesticks" = ESV "lampstands" (Greek "luchniai" — same word as in 1:20). From Zechariah 4. Olive trees supply oil (the Spirit); lampstands give light (witness). Spirit-empowered testimony.
v.8               | KJV         | "which spiritually is called Sodom and Egypt" — Greek "pneumatikōs" — spiritually/symbolically. ESV's "symbolically" clarifies for modern readers. Jerusalem is compared to Sodom (immorality) and Egypt (oppression) — "where also our Lord was crucified" confirms the location.
v.8               | ESV         | "symbolically is called Sodom and Egypt" — Greek "pneumatikōs" — spiritually/symbolically. ESV's "symbolically" clarifies for modern readers. Jerusalem is compared to Sodom (immorality) and Egypt (oppression) — "where also our Lord was crucified" confirms the location.
v.7               | KJV         | "the beast that ascendeth out of the bottomless pit" — Both equivalent. First mention of "the beast" (to be developed in ch.13). He kills the witnesses only after they FINISH their testimony — God's timing.
v.7               | ESV         | "the beast that rises from the bottomless pit" — Both equivalent. First mention of "the beast" (to be developed in ch.13). He kills the witnesses only after they FINISH their testimony — God's timing.
v.15              | KJV         | "The kingdoms of this world are become the kingdoms of our Lord" — ESV follows manuscripts with singular "kingdom" (basileia); KJV uses plural "kingdoms." ESV's singular emphasizes the world as one unified system now transferred to Christ. Either way: all earthly authority transfers to Christ permanently.
v.15              | ESV         | "The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord" — ESV follows manuscripts with singular "kingdom" (basileia); KJV uses plural "kingdoms." ESV's singular emphasizes the world as one unified system now transferred to Christ. Either way: all earthly authority transfers to Christ permanently.
v.19              | KJV         | "the ark of his testament" — Greek "kibōtos tēs diathēkēs" — KJV "testament" = ESV "covenant" (same Greek word "diathēkē"). ESV's "covenant" is more precise in modern English. The ark of the covenant seen in heaven. The earthly ark was a copy; the heavenly original is now revealed. God's faithfulness to His covenant.
v.19              | ESV         | "the ark of his covenant" — Greek "kibōtos tēs diathēkēs" — KJV "testament" = ESV "covenant" (same Greek word "diathēkē"). ESV's "covenant" is more precise in modern English. The ark of the covenant seen in heaven. The earthly ark was a copy; the heavenly original is now revealed. God's faithfulness to His covenant.
v.3               | NET         | "I will grant my two witnesses authority to prophesy for 1,260 days" — "1,260 days" = 42 months = 3.5 years (the symbolic half-week of Daniel 9:27). WEB: "my two witnesses will prophesy one thousand two hundred sixty days." Their identity is debated: Moses/Elijah? the church? two individuals?
v.7               | NET         | "the beast that comes up from the abyss will make war on them and conquer them and kill them" — "the beast" (to thErion) — first appearance of this major figure. WEB: "the beast that comes up out of the abyss." The witnesses are invincible during their ministry (v.5) but killed at its end.
v.11              | NET         | "after three and a half days a breath of life from God entered them and they stood on their feet" — "breath of life" (pneuma zOEs = spirit of life). WEB: "the breath of life from God entered into them." Echoing Ezekiel 37 (dry bones) — resurrection before their enemies' eyes.
v.15              | NET         | "The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ, and he will reign forever and ever" — "kingdom of the world" (hE basileia tou kosmou — singular: one kingdom transferred). WEB: "The kingdom of the world has become the Kingdom of our Lord." KJV: "The kingdoms of this world." The seventh trumpet's climactic announcement.
v.18              | NET         | "the time has come to destroy those who destroy the earth" — "destroy those who destroy" (diaphtheirai tous diaphtheirontas). WEB: "to destroy those who destroy the earth." Judgment matches crime — those who corrupt creation are themselves destroyed.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Two witnesses     | Powerful end-times prophets who testify for 1,260 days.                                                            | 
The beast         | The Antichrist figure who ascends from the abyss. First                                                            | 
Kingdoms become His| The transfer of all earthly authority to Christ. The                                                               | 
Ark of the covenant| God's covenant faithfulness revealed in heaven. The                                                                | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "The kingdoms of this world are become the kingdoms of our Lord" (v.15).
   This is history's destination. Every earthly power will bow to Christ.
   Does this certainty give you confidence when the world seems out of
   control?

2. The two witnesses are killed only AFTER finishing their testimony (v.7).
   God's servants are invincible until their work is done. Does this give
   you courage in your calling?

3. The world celebrates the witnesses' death (v.10) — but God has the last
   word (v.11). When the world celebrates evil, remember: God always has
   the final say. Resurrection follows death.

4. "Thou hast taken to thee thy great power, and hast reigned" (v.17). God
   will assert His power fully. Are you living in anticipation of His
   reign? Are you on the right side of history?

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Revelation Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/revelation/

David Guzik — Revelation 11 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/revelation-11/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

12.ESV:
1. And a great sign appeared in heaven: a woman clothed with the sun, with the moon under her feet, and on her head a crown of twelve stars.
2. She was pregnant and was crying out in birth pains and the agony of giving birth.
3. And another sign appeared in heaven: behold, a great red dragon, with seven heads and ten horns, and on his heads seven diadems.
4. His tail swept down a third of the stars of heaven and cast them to the earth. And the dragon stood before the woman who was about to give birth, so that when she bore her child he might devour it.
5. She gave birth to a male child, one who is to rule all the nations with a rod of iron, but her child was caught up to God and to his throne,
6. and the woman fled into the wilderness, where she has a place prepared by God, in which she is to be nourished for 1,260 days.
7. Now war arose in heaven, Michael and his angels fighting against the dragon. And the dragon and his angels fought back,
8. but he was defeated, and there was no longer any place for them in heaven.
9. And the great dragon was thrown down, that ancient serpent, who is called the devil and Satan, the deceiver of the whole world—he was thrown down to the earth, and his angels were thrown down with him.
10. And I heard a loud voice in heaven, saying, “Now the salvation and the power and the kingdom of our God and the authority of his Christ have come, for the accuser of our brothers has been thrown down, who accuses them day and night before our God.
11. And they have conquered him by the blood of the Lamb and by the word of their testimony, for they loved not their lives even unto death.
12. Therefore, rejoice, O heavens and you who dwell in them! But woe to you, O earth and sea, for the devil has come down to you in great wrath, because he knows that his time is short!”
13. And when the dragon saw that he had been thrown down to the earth, he pursued the woman who had given birth to the male child.
14. But the woman was given the two wings of the great eagle so that she might fly from the serpent into the wilderness, to the place where she is to be nourished for a time, and times, and half a time.
15. The serpent poured water like a river out of his mouth after the woman, to sweep her away with a flood.
16. But the earth came to the help of the woman, and the earth opened its mouth and swallowed the river that the dragon had poured from his mouth.
17. Then the dragon became furious with the woman and went off to make war on the rest of her offspring, on those who keep the commandments of God and hold to the testimony of Jesus. And he stood on the sand of the sea.

12.notes:
Revelation - Chapter 12
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
A woman clothed with the sun gives birth to a male child (Christ), a great red dragon (Satan) is cast out of heaven after war with Michael, and he pursues the woman and makes war with the rest of her offspring who keep God's commandments.


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: The Apostle John (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 12 begins a new section of Revelation (chs.12-14)
that provides the spiritual backstory behind the earthly events. It reveals
the cosmic conflict between Satan and God's people throughout history. Using
vivid symbolic imagery — a woman, a dragon, and a male child — John shows
that the tribulation is the climax of an ancient war that began before human
history.

The Woman (vv.1-2): Clothed with the sun, moon under her feet, crown of
twelve stars (v.1). She is pregnant and in labor (v.2). Identity: Israel —
the nation from which the Messiah came. The imagery echoes Joseph's dream
(Genesis 37:9-10).

The Dragon (vv.3-4): A great red dragon with seven heads, ten horns, seven
crowns (v.3). His tail sweeps a third of heaven's stars (angels) to earth
(v.4). He waits to devour the child at birth (v.4). Identity: Satan (v.9).

The Male Child (v.5): Born to rule all nations with a rod of iron (v.5).
Caught up to God and His throne (v.5). Identity: Jesus Christ — born of
Israel, ascended to heaven.

The Woman's Flight (v.6): She flees to the wilderness — a place prepared by
God — for 1,260 days (v.6). Israel protected during the tribulation.

War in Heaven (vv.7-12): Michael and his angels fight the dragon (v.7). Satan
is cast out of heaven permanently (vv.8-9). He is identified: "that old
serpent, called the Devil, and Satan, which deceiveth the whole world" (v.9).
Victory declared: believers "overcame him by the blood of the Lamb, and by
the word of their testimony; and they loved not their lives unto the death"
(v.11). But woe to earth — Satan has great wrath because his time is short
(v.12).

The Dragon Pursues (vv.13-17): Cast to earth, Satan persecutes the woman
(Israel) (v.13). She is given eagle's wings to escape (v.14). Satan sends a
flood; the earth swallows it (vv.15-16). Enraged, he makes war with "the
remnant of her seed" — those who keep God's commandments and have Jesus's
testimony (v.17).


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/revelation-12/
 Guzik covers the woman (Israel), the dragon (Satan), the male child
 (Christ), the heavenly war, and the threefold victory.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "Overcoming by the Blood" on v.11 (MTP Vol. 11, No. 633)
 "They overcame him — three weapons: (1) The blood of the Lamb — the
 objective ground of victory. Satan's accusations are answered by Christ's
 blood. 'I am guilty — but the blood covers me.' (2) The word of their
 testimony — the subjective confession. They spoke what they believed. (3)
 They loved not their lives unto the death — total commitment. Satan cannot
 defeat someone who is not afraid to die. These three together are
 invincible against the accuser."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1               | KJV         | "wonder" — Greek "sēmeion" — sign. The ESV captures the Greek more precisely. Not just a wonder but a symbolic sign pointing to a greater reality.
v.1               | ESV         | "sign" — Greek "sēmeion" — sign. The ESV captures the Greek more precisely. Not just a wonder but a symbolic sign pointing to a greater reality.
v.2               | KJV         | "travailing in birth, and pained to be delivered" — Both equivalent in meaning. ESV uses more contemporary language.
v.2               | ESV         | "crying out in birth pains and the agony of giving birth" — Both equivalent in meaning. ESV uses more contemporary language.
v.3               | KJV         | "seven heads and ten horns, and seven crowns upon his heads" — ESV uses "diadems" (Greek "diadēmata" — royal crowns) rather than generic "crowns." Seven heads = complete authority. Ten horns = complete power. Seven diadems = claimed sovereignty. Satan's counterfeit of divine authority.
v.3               | ESV         | "seven heads and ten horns, and on his heads seven diadems" — ESV uses "diadems" (Greek "diadēmata" — royal crowns) rather than generic "crowns." Seven heads = complete authority. Ten horns = complete power. Seven diadems = claimed sovereignty. Satan's counterfeit of divine authority.
v.4               | KJV         | "the third part of the stars" — Both equivalent. Angels who followed Satan in his rebellion. One-third of the angelic host became demons.
v.4               | ESV         | "a third of the stars" — Both equivalent. Angels who followed Satan in his rebellion. One-third of the angelic host became demons.
v.5               | KJV         | "a man child, who was to rule all nations with a rod of iron" — Both equivalent. Greek "huion arsen" — a male son.
v.5               | ESV         | "a male child, one who is to rule all the nations with a rod of iron" — Both equivalent. Greek "huion arsen" — a male son.
v.6               | KJV         | "a thousand two hundred and threescore days" — Both identical in meaning. ESV uses numerals for clarity.
v.6               | ESV         | "1,260 days" — Both identical in meaning. ESV uses numerals for clarity.
v.7               | KJV         | "there was war in heaven" — Both equivalent. ESV's "arose" emphasizes the outbreak of conflict.
v.7               | ESV         | "war arose in heaven" — Both equivalent. ESV's "arose" emphasizes the outbreak of conflict.
v.8               | KJV         | "prevailed not; neither was their place found any more in heaven" — ESV is more direct: "defeated" vs. KJV's "prevailed not."
v.8               | ESV         | "he was defeated, and there was no longer any place for them in heaven" — ESV is more direct: "defeated" vs. KJV's "prevailed not."
v.9               | KJV         | "that old serpent, called the Devil, and Satan, which deceiveth the whole world" — ESV uses "ancient" for Greek "archaios" (more precise than "old"). Four names: "the great dragon" (power/terror), "that ancient serpent" (Eden deceiver), "the Devil" (diabolos — slanderer), "Satan" (adversary). Each name reveals an aspect of his character.
v.9               | ESV         | "that ancient serpent, who is called the devil and Satan, the deceiver of the whole world" — ESV uses "ancient" for Greek "archaios" (more precise than "old"). Four names: "the great dragon" (power/terror), "that ancient serpent" (Eden deceiver), "the Devil" (diabolos — slanderer), "Satan" (adversary). Each name reveals an aspect of his character.
v.10              | KJV         | "the accuser of our brethren" — Both equivalent. Greek "katēgōr" — prosecutor, one who brings charges. Satan's primary activity: accusing believers before God. Now cast down — his access to accuse is removed.
v.10              | ESV         | "the accuser of our brothers" — Both equivalent. Greek "katēgōr" — prosecutor, one who brings charges. Satan's primary activity: accusing believers before God. Now cast down — his access to accuse is removed.
v.11              | KJV         | "they overcame him by the blood of the Lamb, and by the word of their testimony; and they loved not their lives unto the death" — ESV uses "conquered" (Greek "nikaō") rather than "overcame." Three means of victory: (1) "blood of the Lamb" — Christ's atonement answers every accusation. (2) "word of their testimony" — bold confession of faith. (3) "loved not their lives unto death" — willingness to die rather than deny.
v.11              | ESV         | "they have conquered him by the blood of the Lamb and by the word of their testimony, for they loved not their lives even unto death" — ESV uses "conquered" (Greek "nikaō") rather than "overcame." Three means of victory: (1) "blood of the Lamb" — Christ's atonement answers every accusation. (2) "word of their testimony" — bold confession of faith. (3) "loved not their lives unto death" — willingness to die rather than deny.
v.12              | KJV         | "he knoweth that he hath but a short time" — Both equivalent. ESV modernizes the phrasing.
v.12              | ESV         | "he knows that his time is short" — Both equivalent. ESV modernizes the phrasing.
v.14              | KJV         | "time, and times, and half a time" — Both equivalent. 1 + 2 + 0.5 = 3.5 years = 42 months = 1,260 days. The second half of the tribulation.
v.14              | ESV         | "a time, and times, and half a time" — Both equivalent. 1 + 2 + 0.5 = 3.5 years = 42 months = 1,260 days. The second half of the tribulation.
v.17              | KJV         | "the remnant of her seed, which keep the commandments of God, and have the testimony of Jesus Christ" — ESV uses "rest of her offspring" for Greek "loipōn tou spermatos" rather than KJV's "remnant of her seed." ESV's "hold to" emphasizes active adherence to testimony.
v.17              | ESV         | "the rest of her offspring, on those who keep the commandments of God and hold to the testimony of Jesus" — ESV uses "rest of her offspring" for Greek "loipōn tou spermatos" rather than KJV's "remnant of her seed." ESV's "hold to" emphasizes active adherence to testimony.
v.1               | NET         | "a woman clothed with the sun, and with the moon under her feet" — "clothed with the sun" (peribeblEmenE ton hElion). WEB: "a woman clothed with the sun." Israel/the church/Mary? — the imagery draws from Genesis 37:9 (Joseph's dream) suggesting Israel giving birth to the Messiah.
v.5               | NET         | "She gave birth to a son, a male child, who is going to rule all the nations with an iron rod" — "iron rod" (rhabdO sidEra — quoting Psalm 2:9). WEB: "a son, a male child, who is to rule all the nations with a rod of iron." The child is Christ — born, ascended (caught up to God's throne).
v.7               | NET         | "Then war broke out in heaven: Michael and his angels fought against the dragon" — "war" (polemos = battle/ warfare). WEB: "There was war in heaven." KJV: "there was war in heaven." Michael leads heaven's armies; Satan and his angels are defeated and cast down permanently.
v.11              | NET         | "they conquered him by the blood of the Lamb and by the word of their testimony, and they did not love their lives so much that they were afraid to die" — three weapons: blood (Christ's sacrifice), testimony (their witness), and willingness to die. WEB: "They overcame him." Martyrdom as victory, not defeat.
v.17              | NET         | "the dragon became enraged at the woman and went away to make war on the rest of her children" — "the rest of her children" (tOn loipOn tou spermatos autEs = the remainder of her seed). WEB: "went away to make war with the rest of her offspring." Believers as targets — those who keep God's commandments and hold to Jesus' testimony.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
The woman         | Israel, the nation from which Christ came. Protected by God                                                        | 
The dragon        | Satan. Identified by four names: dragon, serpent, Devil,                                                           | 
The male child    | Jesus Christ. Born of Israel, destined to rule all nations,                                                        | 
War in heaven     | The cosmic conflict between Michael's angels and Satan's                                                           | 
Accuser cast down | Satan's role as prosecutor of believers is ended. His                                                              | 
Blood of the Lamb | The primary weapon against Satan's accusations. Christ's                                                           | 
Word of testimony | Bold confession of faith. Speaking what you believe                                                                | 
Loved not their lives| Willingness to die rather than deny Christ. The                                                                    | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "They overcame him by the blood of the Lamb" (v.11). When Satan accuses
   you — reminding you of your sin, your failure, your unworthiness — what
   is your answer? The blood. Christ's blood answers every accusation.

2. "The word of their testimony" (v.11). Are you speaking your faith
   boldly? Your testimony is a weapon against the enemy. Silence disarms
   you; confession empowers you.

3. "They loved not their lives unto the death" (v.11). Are you willing to
   lose everything for Christ? Satan cannot defeat someone who isn't afraid
   to die. What are you holding onto that makes you vulnerable?

4. "He knoweth that he hath but a short time" (v.12). Satan's fury is
   fueled by desperation — he knows his time is limited. His attacks are
   intense but temporary. Can you endure knowing his time is short?

5. "The remnant of her seed, which keep the commandments of God, and have
   the testimony of Jesus" (v.17). You are the target of Satan's war. Are
   you keeping God's commandments and holding Jesus's testimony? These
   identify you as the enemy's target — and God's protected.

13.notes:
Revelation - Chapter 13
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Two beasts arise: one from the sea with blasphemous authority over all nations (the Antichrist), and one from the earth who performs signs and enforces the mark of the beast — "his number is Six hundred threescore and six."


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: The Apostle John (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 13 introduces Satan's two primary agents during
the tribulation: the beast from the sea (the Antichrist, vv.1-10) and the
beast from the earth (the False Prophet, vv.11-18). Together with the dragon
(Satan, ch.12), they form an unholy trinity — a counterfeit of Father, Son,
and Spirit. This chapter describes the Antichrist's political/military power
and the False Prophet's religious deception, culminating in the infamous
"mark of the beast" (666).

The Beast from the Sea — The Antichrist (vv.1-10): Rises from the sea (the
nations) with seven heads, ten horns, ten crowns (v.1). Combines features of
Daniel's four beasts: leopard, bear, lion (v.2). The dragon gives him power
(v.2). A fatal wound healed — the world marvels (v.3). He blasphemes God for
42 months (vv.5-6). He makes war with the saints and overcomes them (v.7).
All whose names are not in the Lamb's book worship him (v.8).

The Beast from the Earth — The False Prophet (vv.11-18): Rises from the earth
with lamb-like horns but dragon speech (v.11). He promotes worship of the
first beast (v.12). He performs signs — even fire from heaven (v.13). He
deceives through miracles (v.14). He gives life to the beast's image (v.15).
He enforces the mark — without it, no one can buy or sell (vv.16-17). The
number: 666 (v.18).


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/revelation-13/
 Guzik covers both beasts, the unholy trinity, the mark, 666, and the
 call to patient endurance.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "The Patience of the Saints" on v.10 (MTP Vol. 14, No. 830)
 "Here is the patience and the faith of the saints. In the face of
 overwhelming evil — a beast with global power, forced worship, economic
 control — what is required? Not military victory but patience and faith.
 Endurance, not escape. Trust, not triumph (yet). The saints overcome not
 by fighting the beast but by remaining faithful to the Lamb."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1               | KJV         | "a beast rise up out of the sea, having seven heads and ten horns, and upon his horns ten crowns" — Greek "thērion" — a wild beast (different from "zōon" — living creature in ch.4). ESV uses "diadems" (Greek "diadēmata") for royal crowns. Note the order difference: KJV lists heads first, ESV lists horns first.
v.1               | ESV         | "a beast rising out of the sea, with ten horns and seven heads, with ten diadems on its horns" — Greek "thērion" — a wild beast (different from "zōon" — living creature in ch.4). ESV uses "diadems" (Greek "diadēmata") for royal crowns. Note the order difference: KJV lists heads first, ESV lists horns first.
v.2               | KJV         | "like unto a leopard, and his feet were as the feet of a bear, and his mouth as the mouth of a lion" — Both equivalent. Combines Daniel's four beasts (Dan 7): leopard (Greece — speed), bear (Persia — strength), lion (Babylon — majesty). The final beast incorporates all previous empires' characteristics.
v.2               | ESV         | "like a leopard; its feet were like a bear's, and its mouth was like a lion's mouth" — Both equivalent. Combines Daniel's four beasts (Dan 7): leopard (Greece — speed), bear (Persia — strength), lion (Babylon — majesty). The final beast incorporates all previous empires' characteristics.
v.3               | KJV         | "one of his heads as it were wounded to death; and his deadly wound was healed" — ESV's "mortal wound" is more precise than KJV's "wounded to death." A counterfeit resurrection. The Antichrist mimics Christ's death and resurrection to deceive.
v.3               | ESV         | "One of its heads seemed to have a mortal wound, but its mortal wound was healed" — ESV's "mortal wound" is more precise than KJV's "wounded to death." A counterfeit resurrection. The Antichrist mimics Christ's death and resurrection to deceive.
v.4               | KJV         | "Who is like unto the beast? who is able to make war with him?" — Both equivalent. A blasphemous parody of "Who is like unto thee, O LORD?" (Exodus 15:11).
v.4               | ESV         | "Who is like the beast, and who can fight against it?" — Both equivalent. A blasphemous parody of "Who is like unto thee, O LORD?" (Exodus 15:11).
v.5               | KJV         | "a mouth speaking great things and blasphemies" — ESV adds "haughty" (Greek "megala" — great/boastful things). KJV's "great things" is more literal.
v.5               | ESV         | "a mouth uttering haughty and blasphemous words" — ESV adds "haughty" (Greek "megala" — great/boastful things). KJV's "great things" is more literal.
v.7               | KJV         | "to make war with the saints, and to overcome them" — ESV uses "conquer" (Greek "nikaō") rather than "overcome."
v.7               | ESV         | "to make war on the saints and to conquer them" — ESV uses "conquer" (Greek "nikaō") rather than "overcome."
v.8               | KJV         | "the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world" — Significant structural difference. KJV connects "from the foundation of the world" with the Lamb's slaying (planned before creation). ESV connects it with names written in the book (elected before creation). Greek syntax allows both readings.
v.8               | ESV         | "the book of life of the Lamb who was slain" — Significant structural difference. KJV connects "from the foundation of the world" with the Lamb's slaying (planned before creation). ESV connects it with names written in the book (elected before creation). Greek syntax allows both readings.
v.10              | KJV         | "He that leadeth into captivity shall go into captivity: he that killeth with the sword must be killed with the sword" — ESV follows a different textual variant, reading it as a call to accept God's sovereign will rather than a promise of retribution.
v.10              | ESV         | "If anyone is to be taken captive, to captivity he goes; if anyone is to be slain with the sword, with the sword must he be slain" — ESV follows a different textual variant, reading it as a call to accept God's sovereign will rather than a promise of retribution.
v.11              | KJV         | "two horns like a lamb, and he spake as a dragon" — Both equivalent. Religious appearance (lamb-like) but demonic speech (dragon). The False Prophet looks spiritual but speaks Satan's words.
v.11              | ESV         | "two horns like a lamb and it spoke like a dragon" — Both equivalent. Religious appearance (lamb-like) but demonic speech (dragon). The False Prophet looks spiritual but speaks Satan's words.
v.15              | KJV         | "he had power to give life unto the image of the beast" — ESV uses "breath" (Greek "pneuma") rather than "life." ESV's "allowed" emphasizes God's permissive sovereignty over the beast's actions.
v.15              | ESV         | "it was allowed to give breath to the image of the beast" — ESV uses "breath" (Greek "pneuma") rather than "life." ESV's "allowed" emphasizes God's permissive sovereignty over the beast's actions.
v.16              | KJV         | "to receive a mark in their right hand, or in their foreheads" — Greek "charagma" — a stamp, brand, engraving. Like a slave's brand or a coin's imprint. KJV says "in" (suggesting embedded); ESV says "on" (suggesting external). Greek "epi" can mean either.
v.16              | ESV         | "to be marked on the right hand or the forehead" — Greek "charagma" — a stamp, brand, engraving. Like a slave's brand or a coin's imprint. KJV says "in" (suggesting embedded); ESV says "on" (suggesting external). Greek "epi" can mean either.
v.18              | KJV         | "Six hundred threescore and six" — Both identical in value. ESV uses numerals. Interpretations: (1) Nero Caesar in Hebrew gematria, (2) symbolic: 6 = man's number (one short of 7/perfection), tripled for emphasis — the ultimate human pretension to deity, (3) a future identification system.
v.18              | ESV         | "666" — Both identical in value. ESV uses numerals. Interpretations: (1) Nero Caesar in Hebrew gematria, (2) symbolic: 6 = man's number (one short of 7/perfection), tripled for emphasis — the ultimate human pretension to deity, (3) a future identification system.
v.1               | NET         | "I saw a beast coming up out of the sea. It had ten horns and seven heads" — "out of the sea" (ek tEs thalassEs). WEB: "a beast coming up out of the sea." The sea represents chaos/nations (17:15). Daniel 7's four beasts combined into one — a composite of all human opposition to God.
v.4               | NET         | "they worshiped the dragon because he gave ruling authority to the beast" — "worshiped the dragon" (prosekynEsan tO drakonti). WEB: "they worshiped the dragon." KJV: "they worshipped the dragon." Satan's ultimate goal achieved — worship directed to himself through a human proxy.
v.8               | NET         | "all those who live on the earth will worship the beast, everyone whose name has not been written since the foundation of the world in the book of life" — "since the foundation" (apo katabolEs kosmou). WEB: "from the foundation of the world." Names written BEFORE creation — divine foreknowledge and election.
v.10              | NET         | "This requires steadfast endurance and faith from the saints" — "steadfast endurance" (hypomonE = patient perseverance under pressure). WEB: "Here is the perseverance and the faith of the saints." In the face of the beast's power, endurance IS victory.
v.18              | NET         | "let the one who has insight calculate the beast's number, for it is man's number, and his number is 666" — "man's number" (arithmos anthrOpou). WEB: "his number is six hundred sixty-six." Some manuscripts read 616. Gematria (number = letters) most commonly yields "Nero Caesar" in Hebrew. Ultimate meaning debated across millennia.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Beast from the sea| The Antichrist. A political/military leader empowered                                                              | 
Beast from the earth| The False Prophet. A religious leader who promotes                                                                 | 
Unholy trinity    | Satan (counterfeit Father), Antichrist (counterfeit Son),                                                          | 
Mark of the beast | A mark of allegiance to the Antichrist. Required for                                                               | 
Book of life      | God's register of the redeemed. Those written in it will                                                           | 
Patience and faith| What saints need during the beast's reign. Not military                                                            | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "The dragon gave him his power" (v.2). Behind every human evil is a
   spiritual reality. Do you recognize the spiritual forces behind worldly
   systems that oppose God?

2. "All that dwell upon the earth shall worship him, whose names are not
   written in the book of life" (v.8). Only those in the Lamb's book
   resist. Is your name written there? What gives you confidence?

3. "Here is the patience and the faith of the saints" (v.10). In the face
   of overwhelming evil, God calls for patience and faith — not panic. How
   do you respond when evil seems to be winning?

4. The False Prophet looks like a lamb but speaks like a dragon (v.11). Not
   everything that looks spiritual IS spiritual. How do you discern between
   genuine and counterfeit spirituality?

5. The mark represents total allegiance to a system opposed to God. What
   "marks" of worldly allegiance are you tempted to accept — compromises
   that give you access to the world's system at the cost of faithfulness?

14.ESV:
1. Then I looked, and behold, on Mount Zion stood the Lamb, and with him 144,000 who had his name and his Father’s name written on their foreheads.
2. And I heard a voice from heaven like the roar of many waters and like the sound of loud thunder. The voice I heard was like the sound of harpists playing on their harps,
3. and they were singing a new song before the throne and before the four living creatures and before the elders. No one could learn that song except the 144,000 who had been redeemed from the earth.
4. It is these who have not defiled themselves with women, for they are virgins. It is these who follow the Lamb wherever he goes. These have been redeemed from mankind as firstfruits for God and the Lamb,
5. and in their mouth no lie was found, for they are blameless.
6. Then I saw another angel flying directly overhead, with an eternal gospel to proclaim to those who dwell on earth, to every nation and tribe and language and people.
7. And he said with a loud voice, “Fear God and give him glory, because the hour of his judgment has come, and worship him who made heaven and earth, the sea and the springs of water.”
8. Another angel, a second, followed, saying, “Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great, she who made all nations drink the wine of the passion of her sexual immorality.”
9. And another angel, a third, followed them, saying with a loud voice, “If anyone worships the beast and its image and receives a mark on his forehead or on his hand,
10. he also will drink the wine of God’s wrath, poured full strength into the cup of his anger, and he will be tormented with fire and sulfur in the presence of the holy angels and in the presence of the Lamb.
11. And the smoke of their torment goes up forever and ever, and they have no rest, day or night, these worshipers of the beast and its image, and whoever receives the mark of its name.”
12. Here is a call for the endurance of the saints, those who keep the commandments of God and their faith in Jesus.
13. And I heard a voice from heaven saying, “Write this: Blessed are the dead who die in the Lord from now on.” “Blessed indeed,” says the Spirit, “that they may rest from their labors, for their deeds follow them!”
14. Then I looked, and behold, a white cloud, and seated on the cloud one like a son of man, with a golden crown on his head, and a sharp sickle in his hand.
15. And another angel came out of the temple, calling with a loud voice to him who sat on the cloud, “Put in your sickle, and reap, for the hour to reap has come, for the harvest of the earth is fully ripe.”
16. So he who sat on the cloud swung his sickle across the earth, and the earth was reaped.
17. Then another angel came out of the temple in heaven, and he too had a sharp sickle.
18. And another angel came out from the altar, the angel who has authority over the fire, and he called with a loud voice to the one who had the sharp sickle, “Put in your sickle and gather the clusters from the vine of the earth, for its grapes are ripe.”
19. So the angel swung his sickle across the earth and gathered the grape harvest of the earth and threw it into the great winepress of the wrath of God.
20. And the winepress was trodden outside the city, and blood flowed from the winepress, as high as a horse’s bridle, for 1,600 stadia.

14.notes:
Revelation - Chapter 14
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The Lamb stands on Mount Zion with the 144,000, three angels announce eternal gospel and Babylon's fall, and the harvest of the earth begins — "Blessed are the dead which die in the Lord from henceforth."


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: The Apostle John (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 14 provides a preview of coming events — a series
of visions showing the ultimate outcome before the bowl judgments begin. It
contrasts the 144,000 with the Lamb (vv.1-5) against those who take the
beast's mark (vv.9-11). Three angels announce messages (vv.6-13), and two
harvests depict final judgment (vv.14-20). This chapter assures believers:
despite chapter 13's terror, God wins.

The 144,000 with the Lamb (vv.1-5): The Lamb stands on Mount Zion with
144,000 who have the Father's name on their foreheads (v.1). They sing a new
song only they can learn (vv.2-3). They are pure, follow the Lamb, are
redeemed as firstfruits, and are without guile or fault (vv.4-5).

Three Angelic Announcements (vv.6-13):
- Angel 1 (vv.6-7): The "everlasting gospel" proclaimed to all nations:
 "Fear God, and give glory to him; for the hour of his judgment is come."
- Angel 2 (v.8): "Babylon is fallen, is fallen" — the world system's doom
 announced.
- Angel 3 (vv.9-11): Warning: those who worship the beast and take his mark
 will drink God's wrath "without mixture" — eternal torment.

"Blessed are the dead which die in the Lord" (v.13) — they rest from labor
and their works follow them.

Two Harvests (vv.14-20): The grain harvest — the Son of man reaps the earth
(vv.14-16). The grape harvest — grapes gathered and thrown into "the great
winepress of the wrath of God" (vv.17-20). Blood flows for 1,600 furlongs
(about 200 miles) — the length of Israel.


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/revelation-14/
 Guzik covers the 144,000, the three angels, the harvest imagery, and the
 winepress of God's wrath.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "Blessed Are the Dead" on v.13 (MTP Vol. 11, No. 632)
 "Blessed are the dead which die IN THE LORD. Not all dead are blessed —
 only those who die in the Lord. And their blessedness: they REST from their
 labours — no more toil, no more struggle, no more weariness. And their
 works DO FOLLOW them — not precede them (works don't earn heaven) but
 follow them (works are rewarded in heaven). Die in the Lord and you rest;
 your works are not lost but rewarded."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1               | KJV         | "having his Father's name written in their foreheads" — ESV includes both the Lamb's name AND the Father's name (following better manuscript evidence). Contrast with 13:16-17 (the beast's mark). God's people bear God's name; the world bears the beast's mark.
v.1               | ESV         | "who had his name and his Father's name written on their foreheads" — ESV includes both the Lamb's name AND the Father's name (following better manuscript evidence). Contrast with 13:16-17 (the beast's mark). God's people bear God's name; the world bears the beast's mark.
v.2               | KJV         | "the voice of many waters... the voice of a great thunder... the voice of harpers harping with their harps" — Both equivalent. ESV uses "roar" for greater intensity.
v.2               | ESV         | "the roar of many waters... the sound of loud thunder... the sound of harpists playing on their harps" — Both equivalent. ESV uses "roar" for greater intensity.
v.3               | KJV         | "no man could learn that song but the hundred and forty and four thousand, which were redeemed from the earth" — Both equivalent. ESV modernizes the phrasing.
v.3               | ESV         | "No one could learn that song except the 144,000 who had been redeemed from the earth" — Both equivalent. ESV modernizes the phrasing.
v.4               | KJV         | "These are they which were not defiled with women; for they are virgins" — Both equivalent. Greek "parthenoi" — can be literal (celibate) or figurative (spiritually pure, not defiled by idolatry/the beast system). "Firstfruits" — the first and best of the harvest, dedicated to God.
v.4               | ESV         | "It is these who have not defiled themselves with women, for they are virgins" — Both equivalent. Greek "parthenoi" — can be literal (celibate) or figurative (spiritually pure, not defiled by idolatry/the beast system). "Firstfruits" — the first and best of the harvest, dedicated to God.
v.5               | KJV         | "in their mouth was found no guile: for they are without fault before the throne of God" — ESV uses "lie" for Greek "pseudos" (more precise than KJV's "guile"). ESV omits "before the throne of God" (absent in best manuscripts). ESV's "blameless" = Greek "amōmoi" (without blemish).
v.5               | ESV         | "in their mouth no lie was found, for they are blameless" — ESV uses "lie" for Greek "pseudos" (more precise than KJV's "guile"). ESV omits "before the throne of God" (absent in best manuscripts). ESV's "blameless" = Greek "amōmoi" (without blemish).
v.6               | KJV         | "the everlasting gospel" — KJV uses definite article "the"; ESV uses indefinite "an." Greek has no article here. Both translate "euangelion aiōnion."
v.6               | ESV         | "an eternal gospel" — KJV uses definite article "the"; ESV uses indefinite "an." Greek has no article here. Both translate "euangelion aiōnion."
v.8               | KJV         | "Babylon is fallen, is fallen, that great city, because she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication" — ESV uses "passion" (Greek "thumos" — fury/passion) and "sexual immorality" (Greek "porneia") rather than KJV's "wrath" and "fornication." "Babylon" represents the world system opposed to God.
v.8               | ESV         | "Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great, she who made all nations drink the wine of the passion of her sexual immorality" — ESV uses "passion" (Greek "thumos" — fury/passion) and "sexual immorality" (Greek "porneia") rather than KJV's "wrath" and "fornication." "Babylon" represents the world system opposed to God.
v.10              | KJV         | "poured out without mixture into the cup of his indignation" — Greek "akratou" — unmixed, undiluted. ESV's "full strength" captures the meaning well. In the ancient world, wine was usually diluted with water. God's wrath will be full strength — no mercy mixed in.
v.10              | ESV         | "poured full strength into the cup of his anger" — Greek "akratou" — unmixed, undiluted. ESV's "full strength" captures the meaning well. In the ancient world, wine was usually diluted with water. God's wrath will be full strength — no mercy mixed in.
v.11              | KJV         | "the smoke of their torment ascendeth up for ever and ever" — Both equivalent.
v.11              | ESV         | "the smoke of their torment goes up forever and ever" — Both equivalent.
v.12              | KJV         | "Here is the patience of the saints: here are they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus" — ESV's "endurance" (Greek "hypomonē") is more precise than KJV's "patience." ESV's "faith in Jesus" interprets the genitive differently than KJV's "faith of Jesus."
v.12              | ESV         | "Here is a call for the endurance of the saints, those who keep the commandments of God and their faith in Jesus" — ESV's "endurance" (Greek "hypomonē") is more precise than KJV's "patience." ESV's "faith in Jesus" interprets the genitive differently than KJV's "faith of Jesus."
v.13              | KJV         | "their works do follow them" — ESV uses "deeds" (Greek "erga") rather than "works." Both equivalent. Works don't precede (earning entrance) but follow (receiving reward). Salvation is by grace; reward is by works.
v.13              | ESV         | "their deeds follow them" — ESV uses "deeds" (Greek "erga") rather than "works." Both equivalent. Works don't precede (earning entrance) but follow (receiving reward). Salvation is by grace; reward is by works.
v.14              | KJV         | "one sat like unto the Son of man" — Both equivalent. ESV's lowercase "son of man" reflects the Greek (no definite article here), though the reference to Christ is clear from Daniel 7:13.
v.14              | ESV         | "seated on the cloud one like a son of man" — Both equivalent. ESV's lowercase "son of man" reflects the Greek (no definite article here), though the reference to Christ is clear from Daniel 7:13.
v.20              | KJV         | "a thousand and six hundred furlongs" — Both equivalent. Greek "stadiōn" — about 200 miles. The approximate length of Israel. Blood reaching horse bridles = total, devastating judgment.
v.20              | ESV         | "1,600 stadia" — Both equivalent. Greek "stadiōn" — about 200 miles. The approximate length of Israel. Blood reaching horse bridles = total, devastating judgment.
v.1               | NET         | "the Lamb was standing on Mount Zion, and with him were one hundred and forty-four thousand" — "Mount Zion" (to oros SiOn) — heavenly Zion, in contrast to the beast's earthly domain. WEB: "the Lamb standing on Mount Zion." The sealed ones of chapter 7 appear here victorious.
v.6               | NET         | "an eternal gospel to proclaim to those who live on the earth — to every nation, tribe, language, and people" — "eternal gospel" (euangelion aiOnion). WEB: "an eternal Good News." Even at the end, the gospel is proclaimed — God's mercy persists until the last possible moment.
v.8               | NET         | "Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great" — "fallen, fallen" (epesen epesen — doubled for emphasis/certainty). WEB: "Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great." Echoing Isaiah 21:9 — the announcement precedes the detailed destruction (chapters 17-18).
v.13              | NET         | "Blessed are the dead who die in the Lord from this point on" — "from this point on" (ap' arti = from now on). WEB: "Blessed are the dead who die in the Lord from now on." KJV: "Blessed are the dead which die in the Lord from henceforth." The second of seven beatitudes in Revelation.
v.20              | NET         | "the winepress was stomped outside the city, and blood poured out of the winepress up to the horses' bridles for a distance of almost two hundred miles" — "two hundred miles" (stadiOn chiliOn hexakosiOn = 1,600 stadia). WEB: "one thousand six hundred stadia." Hyperbolic imagery of devastating judgment — rivers of blood.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Everlasting gospel| The eternal message: fear God, give Him glory, worship                                                             | 
Babylon is fallen | The announcement of the world system's doom. Political,                                                            | 
Without mixture   | God's wrath undiluted. Full strength, no mercy mixed in.                                                           | 
Blessed are the dead in the Lord| Those who die in Christ rest from labor                                                                            | 
Winepress of wrath| Final judgment depicted as grapes crushed in a press.                                                              | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Having his Father's name written in their foreheads" (v.1). Whose name
   do you bear — God's or the world's? Your identity is marked by your
   allegiance.

2. "Fear God, and give glory to him" (v.7). The eternal gospel is simple:
   fear God, glorify Him, worship the Creator. Is this the center of your
   life?

3. "Here is the patience of the saints" (v.12). In the face of the beast's
   power and God's coming wrath, saints are called to patient endurance.
   Are you enduring faithfully?

4. "Blessed are the dead which die in the Lord" (v.13). Death in Christ is
   not tragedy but blessing — rest and reward. Does this change how you
   view death — yours or others'?

5. The two harvests (vv.14-20) show that judgment is coming — both for
   salvation (grain) and for wrath (grapes). Which harvest will you be part
   of?

15.notes:
Revelation - Chapter 15
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Seven angels receive seven bowls of God's final wrath, victorious saints sing the song of Moses and the Lamb, and the temple in heaven is filled with smoke from God's glory — no one can enter until the seven plagues are completed.


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: The Apostle John (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 15 is the shortest chapter in Revelation (8
verses) and serves as the prelude to the seven bowl judgments (ch.16). It
presents a scene of worship in heaven before the final, most severe judgments
are poured out. The victorious saints sing, and seven angels receive seven
bowls of God's wrath. This chapter balances terror (final plagues) with
triumph (the overcomers' song).

The Seven Last Plagues (v.1): Seven angels with seven plagues — "in them is
filled up the wrath of God." These are the final, most intense judgments.
God's wrath reaches its completion.

The Overcomers' Song (vv.2-4): Those who conquered the beast stand on a sea
of glass mixed with fire (v.2). They sing "the song of Moses... and the song
of the Lamb" (v.3) — celebrating God's works as "great and marvellous," His
ways as "just and true" (v.3). "Who shall not fear thee, O Lord?" (v.4). All
nations will worship because His judgments are revealed (v.4).

The Angels with Bowls (vv.5-8): The heavenly temple opens (v.5). Seven angels
emerge in pure white with golden sashes (v.6). One of the living creatures
gives them seven golden bowls "full of the wrath of God" (v.7). The temple
fills with smoke from God's glory — no one can enter until the plagues are
complete (v.8).


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/revelation-15/
 Guzik covers the last plagues, the overcomers' song, the Song of Moses
 and the Lamb, and the temple filled with glory.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "Just and True Are Thy Ways" on v.3 (MTP Vol. 22, No. 1300)
 "Just and true are thy ways — even in judgment. Even when wrath is poured
 out, God's ways are just (fair) and true (right). The overcomers don't
 question God's judgments — they celebrate them. They have seen evil's full
 horror and they know: God's wrath is righteous. His judgments are not
 excessive — they are exactly what rebellion deserves."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1               | KJV         | "in them is filled up the wrath of God" — Greek "etelesthē" — completed, finished, brought to an end. ESV's "finished" captures the finality more clearly. God's wrath reaches its full expression in these final plagues.
v.1               | ESV         | "with them the wrath of God is finished" — Greek "etelesthē" — completed, finished, brought to an end. ESV's "finished" captures the finality more clearly. God's wrath reaches its full expression in these final plagues.
v.2               | KJV         | "them that had gotten the victory over the beast" — ESV's "conquered" (Greek "nikōntas") is more forceful than KJV's "gotten the victory." KJV: "sea of glass mingled with fire" / ESV: "a sea of glass mingled with fire" — Both equivalent. In ch.4 it was calm crystal; now it's mixed with fire — reflecting the judgment about to be poured out.
v.2               | ESV         | "those who had conquered the beast" — ESV's "conquered" (Greek "nikōntas") is more forceful than KJV's "gotten the victory." KJV: "sea of glass mingled with fire" / ESV: "a sea of glass mingled with fire" — Both equivalent. In ch.4 it was calm crystal; now it's mixed with fire — reflecting the judgment about to be poured out.
v.3               | KJV         | "Great and marvellous are thy works, Lord God Almighty; just and true are thy ways, thou King of saints" — Key difference: KJV has "King of saints" while ESV has "King of the nations" (Greek "ethnōn" — nations; some manuscripts read "hagiōn" — saints). ESV's "amazing" for Greek "thaumasta" vs. KJV's "marvellous." One song with two themes: deliverance (Moses at the Red Sea) and redemption (the Lamb at the cross).
v.3               | ESV         | "Great and amazing are your deeds, O Lord God the Almighty! Just and true are your ways, O King of the nations!" — Key difference: KJV has "King of saints" while ESV has "King of the nations" (Greek "ethnōn" — nations; some manuscripts read "hagiōn" — saints). ESV's "amazing" for Greek "thaumasta" vs. KJV's "marvellous." One song with two themes: deliverance (Moses at the Red Sea) and redemption (the Lamb at the cross).
v.4               | KJV         | "Who shall not fear thee, O Lord, and glorify thy name? for thou only art holy" — Both equivalent. Greek "monos hosios" — You alone are holy. God's unique, unshared holiness.
v.4               | ESV         | "Who will not fear, O Lord, and glorify your name? For you alone are holy" — Both equivalent. Greek "monos hosios" — You alone are holy. God's unique, unshared holiness.
v.5               | KJV         | "the temple of the tabernacle of the testimony in heaven was opened" — ESV uses "sanctuary" and "tent of witness" for Greek "naos" and "skēnē tou marturiou." KJV's "temple of the tabernacle of the testimony" is more literal but less clear.
v.5               | ESV         | "the sanctuary of the tent of witness in heaven was opened" — ESV uses "sanctuary" and "tent of witness" for Greek "naos" and "skēnē tou marturiou." KJV's "temple of the tabernacle of the testimony" is more literal but less clear.
v.6               | KJV         | "clothed in pure and white linen, and having their breasts girded with golden girdles" — ESV modernizes "girdles" to "sashes." Both describe priestly garments reflecting holiness and authority.
v.6               | ESV         | "clothed in pure, bright linen, with golden sashes around their chests" — ESV modernizes "girdles" to "sashes." Both describe priestly garments reflecting holiness and authority.
v.7               | KJV         | "seven golden vials full of the wrath of God" — ESV's "bowls" (Greek "phialas" — broad, shallow bowls) is more accurate than KJV's "vials" (which suggests narrow containers). These are libation bowls — designed to pour out quickly.
v.7               | ESV         | "seven golden bowls full of the wrath of God" — ESV's "bowls" (Greek "phialas" — broad, shallow bowls) is more accurate than KJV's "vials" (which suggests narrow containers). These are libation bowls — designed to pour out quickly.
v.8               | KJV         | "no man was able to enter into the temple" — Both equivalent. During the outpouring of final wrath, even heaven's temple is inaccessible. God's glory and wrath fill the space completely. Intercession is suspended — judgment must run its course.
v.8               | ESV         | "no one could enter the sanctuary" — Both equivalent. During the outpouring of final wrath, even heaven's temple is inaccessible. God's glory and wrath fill the space completely. Intercession is suspended — judgment must run its course.
v.2               | NET         | "those who had conquered the beast and his image... standing beside the sea of glass, holding harps of God" — "conquered" (nikOntas = those overcoming/victorious). WEB: "those who overcame the beast." Victory through faithfulness (not military power) — they stood firm and now they stand glorified.
v.3               | NET         | "They sang the song of Moses the servant of God and the song of the Lamb" — "song of Moses AND the Lamb" — old and new covenants united in one worship. WEB: "the song of Moses, the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb." Exodus 15 (Red Sea deliverance) meets the Lamb's redemption.
v.3               | NET         | "Great and astounding are your deeds, Lord God, the All-Powerful!" — "astounding" (thaumasta = marvelous/ wonderful). WEB: "Great and marvelous are your works." KJV: "Great and marvellous are thy works." The content echoes multiple OT psalms (Psalms 111:2, 139:14, 145:17).
v.4               | NET         | "Who will not fear you, O Lord, and glorify your name? Because you alone are holy" — "you alone are holy" (monos hosios = uniquely set apart). WEB: "for you only are holy." After all the judgments, the conclusion: God is righteous, God alone is holy, all nations will worship.
v.8               | NET         | "no one could enter the temple until the seven plagues from the seven angels were completed" — "no one could enter" (oudeis edynato eiselthein). WEB: "No one was able to enter into the temple." Access blocked during wrath — intercession is suspended while justice runs its full course.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Seven last plagues| The final, most severe judgments. God's wrath completed                                                            | 
Song of Moses and the Lamb| A victory song celebrating God's deliverance                                                                       | 
Sea of glass with fire| The heavenly expanse now reflecting judgment's fire.                                                               | 
Just and true     | God's character in judgment. His wrath is fair (just) and                                                          | 
Temple filled with smoke| God's glory so intense that the temple is                                                                          | 
Overcomers        | Those who conquered the beast by refusing his mark. They                                                           | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Great and marvellous are thy works... just and true are thy ways"
   (v.3). Can you say this about God's ways — even the difficult ones? Even
   His judgments are just and true. Do you trust His character?

2. "Who shall not fear thee?" (v.4). God's holiness demands reverence. Do
   you approach God with appropriate awe? Or has familiarity bred
   casualness?

3. The overcomers conquered the beast (v.2). They refused to compromise
   even at the cost of their lives. What would you refuse to compromise on,
   even if it cost you everything?

4. "In them is filled up the wrath of God" (v.1). God's wrath has a limit —
   it will be "filled up" and finished. Even judgment has an end. How does
   this shape your understanding of God's character?

5. Before judgment falls, heaven worships (vv.2-4). Worship precedes and
   frames everything God does. Is worship the framework of your life?

16.ESV:
1. Then I heard a loud voice from the temple telling the seven angels, “Go and pour out on the earth the seven bowls of the wrath of God.”
2. So the first angel went and poured out his bowl on the earth, and harmful and painful sores came upon the people who bore the mark of the beast and worshiped its image.
3. The second angel poured out his bowl into the sea, and it became like the blood of a corpse, and every living thing died that was in the sea.
4. The third angel poured out his bowl into the rivers and the springs of water, and they became blood.
5. And I heard the angel in charge of the waters say, “Just are you, O Holy One, who is and who was, for you brought these judgments.
6. For they have shed the blood of saints and prophets, and you have given them blood to drink. It is what they deserve!”
7. And I heard the altar saying, “Yes, Lord God the Almighty, true and just are your judgments!”
8. The fourth angel poured out his bowl on the sun, and it was allowed to scorch people with fire.
9. They were scorched by the fierce heat, and they cursed the name of God who had power over these plagues. They did not repent and give him glory.
10. The fifth angel poured out his bowl on the throne of the beast, and its kingdom was plunged into darkness. People gnawed their tongues in anguish
11. and cursed the God of heaven for their pain and sores. They did not repent of their deeds.
12. The sixth angel poured out his bowl on the great river Euphrates, and its water was dried up, to prepare the way for the kings from the east.
13. And I saw, coming out of the mouth of the dragon and out of the mouth of the beast and out of the mouth of the false prophet, three unclean spirits like frogs.
14. For they are demonic spirits, performing signs, who go abroad to the kings of the whole world, to assemble them for battle on the great day of God the Almighty.
15. (“Behold, I am coming like a thief! Blessed is the one who stays awake, keeping his garments on, that he may not go about naked and be seen exposed!”)
16. And they assembled them at the place that in Hebrew is called Armageddon.
17. The seventh angel poured out his bowl into the air, and a loud voice came out of the temple, from the throne, saying, “It is done!”
18. And there were flashes of lightning, rumblings, peals of thunder, and a great earthquake such as there had never been since man was on the earth, so great was that earthquake.
19. The great city was split into three parts, and the cities of the nations fell, and God remembered Babylon the great, to make her drain the cup of the wine of the fury of his wrath.
20. And every island fled away, and no mountains were to be found.
21. And great hailstones, about one hundred pounds each, fell from heaven on people; and they cursed God for the plague of the hail, because the plague was so severe.
16.KJV:
1. And I heard a great voice out of the temple saying to the seven angels, Go your ways, and pour out the vials of the wrath of God upon the earth.
2. And the first went, and poured out his vial upon the earth; and there fell a noisome and grievous sore upon the men which had the mark of the beast, and upon them which worshipped his image.
3. And the second angel poured out his vial upon the sea; and it became as the blood of a dead man: and every living soul died in the sea.
4. And the third angel poured out his vial upon the rivers and fountains of waters; and they became blood.
5. And I heard the angel of the waters say, Thou art righteous, O Lord, which art, and wast, and shalt be, because thou hast judged thus.
6. For they have shed the blood of saints and prophets, and thou hast given them blood to drink; for they are worthy.
7. And I heard another out of the altar say, Even so, Lord God Almighty, true and righteous are thy judgments.
8. And the fourth angel poured out his vial upon the sun; and power was given unto him to scorch men with fire.
9. And men were scorched with great heat, and blasphemed the name of God, which hath power over these plagues: and they repented not to give him glory.

1. Then I heard a loud voice from the temple declaring to the seven angels: “Go and pour out on the earth the seven bowls containing God’s wrath.”
